episodes.summary: English words ending in -al tend to come from Latin and can be converted to Spanish by pronouncing them with Spanish phonetics and stressing the final syllable.
episodes.rule: Words ending in -al in English are often the same in Spanish, just pronounced with Spanish vowels and stress on the final syllable.
episodes.notes: Pronounce each vowel as written: a=/ah/, e=/eh/, i=/ee/, o=/oh/, u=/oo/. Cut out English sounds that come from nowhere (like the y in 'annual').

episodes.summary: Spanish vowels always have the same sound: a=/ah/, e=/eh/, i=/ee/, o=/oh/, u=/oo/. Unlike English where vowels have many sounds, Spanish is phonetic.
episodes.rule: a=/ah/, e=/eh/, i=/ee/, o=/oh/, u=/oo/ — always consistent.
episodes.notes: Spanish is much more phonetic than English. Pronounce letters as they are written.

episodes.summary: The word 'es' means is, and also covers he is, she is, it is, and you are (formal). No subject pronoun is needed.
episodes.rule: 'Es' = is / he is / she is / it is / you (formal) are.

episodes.summary: Continued practice using 'es' in sentences with new adjectives and adverbs.
To negate, place 'no' before the verb. 'No' means both 'no' and 'not/don't'.
episodes.rule: Place 'no' before the verb to negate: 'No es' = it is not.

episodes.summary: English has a huge amount of Latin vocabulary that can be converted to Spanish with simple rules, giving access to around 3,000 words.
episodes.notes: Spanish is practically Modern Latin — a 'street Latin' that evolved into Spanish.

episodes.summary: English words ending in -ant or -ent can be converted to Spanish by adding an -e on the end, making them -ante or -ente.
episodes.rule: -ant → -ante, -ent → -ente in Spanish.

episodes.summary: Adding -mente to an adjective creates an adverb, equivalent to -ly in English.
episodes.rule: Adjective + -mente = adverb (like English -ly).
episodes.notes: 'Normalmente' can be used like 'usually'. 'Naturalmente' can be used like 'obviously'.

episodes.summary: When a convertible English word has a /j/ sound (not just the letter g), it becomes a /kh/ (fricative) sound in Spanish.
episodes.rule: The /j/ sound in English cognates becomes /kh/ (throat friction) in Spanish. A hard /g/ stays as /g/.
episodes.notes: Argentina is a good hook to remember this rule — the g in Argentina has the /kh/ sound.

episodes.summary: English words ending -ible or -able can be converted to Spanish by pronouncing them syllable by syllable with the accent second from the end.
episodes.rule: -ible/-able words convert to Spanish with same spelling, pronounced syllable by syllable with penultimate stress.
episodes.notes: This works when -able/-ible is truly part of the word (not coined words like 'doable').

episodes.summary: English words ending -ion are the same in Spanish with the accent on the end. When the English has -tion, it becomes -ción in Spanish.
episodes.rule: -ion words are the same in Spanish with final stress. -tion becomes -ción (pronounced /sión/ in Latin America, /thión/ in most of Spain).
episodes.notes: This is described as the teacher's favourite rule due to its productivity.

episodes.summary: For words ending -ación, remove -ción and add -r to get the verb. This gives access to roughly 1,000 verbs.
episodes.rule: -ación → remove -ción, add -r → -ar verb. E.g., preparación → preparar.
episodes.notes: Always take the long route: English word → -ación noun → -ar verb. Don't jump directly from English verb to Spanish.

episodes.summary: Continued practice of the -ación to -ar derivation rule with new words. Also introduced the concept that experimentar means both 'to experiment' and 'to experience'.
episodes.notes: Take the long route syllable by syllable. Don't jump from English verb to Spanish — go through the -ación noun first.

episodes.summary: Quiero means 'I want'. Derived from 'te quiero' (I love you, literally 'I want you') by removing 'te'.
episodes.notes: 'Te quiero' literally means 'I want you' and is used for 'I love you' (casual). 'Te amo' is much more serious.

episodes.summary: The word for 'me' in Spanish is 'me', pronounced as written. It also serves as 'myself' in reflexive constructions.
episodes.rule: 'Me' attaches to the end of infinitives: prepararme = to prepare myself.

examples.english: I want to prepare myself / I want to get ready

episodes.notes: Latin words sound formal in English but are everyday language in Spanish.

episodes.summary: Positive language transfer is when English knowledge helps with Spanish (like cognates). Negative language transfer is when English habits interfere with correct Spanish.
episodes.notes: Con = with, firma = signature. So 'confirm' means 'with signature'.

episodes.summary: The word for 'I' is 'yo' but it's only used for emphasis. The verb form alone conveys 'I'. Word order for negation: yo no quiero.
episodes.rule: 'Yo' is only used for emphasis. Normal order: Yo no quiero (I don't want). 'No' comes before the verb, not before 'yo'.

episodes.summary: Lo means 'it' or 'him'. It attaches to the end of infinitives.
episodes.rule: 'Lo' attaches to end of infinitives. Lo = it / him.

episodes.summary: Te is used as informal 'you' (object). It attaches to the end of infinitives like -lo and -me.

examples.english: I don't want to oblige you
I don't want to provoke you

episodes.summary: -ence in English becomes -encia in Spanish, and -ance becomes -ancia.
episodes.rule: -ence → -encia, -ance → -ancia.

episodes.summary: Initial r or double rr inside a word is a rolling/trilled r. Single r between vowels is a softer tap. Don't stress about it — it will come naturally.
episodes.rule: Initial r = rolling r. Double rr inside a word = rolling r. Single r between vowels = tap.
episodes.notes: The teacher couldn't pronounce the rolling r for a long time and it came naturally while practicing in Peru.

episodes.summary: Intento means 'I try' or 'I am trying'. Derived from understanding the connection to 'intention'. The -o ending shows it's the 'I' form.
episodes.rule: Intento = I try / I am trying. The -o ending indicates first person (I).

examples.english: I am trying to publish it
I am trying to administrate it
I am trying to imitate him/it

episodes.notes: Connected to 'intention/intención' but there's no formal -ación rule here since it doesn't end in -ation.

episodes.summary: H is completely silent in Spanish. You must not even think it, as it tends to slip out. The exception is ch which is pronounced /ch/ as in English.
episodes.rule: H is always silent in Spanish. Exception: ch = /ch/.
episodes.notes: Think of the word as beginning with the vowel, not with h.

episodes.summary: Ahora means 'now'. It's composed of 'a' (to) + 'hora' (hour), literally 'to the hour'.
episodes.notes: Spelled a-h-o-r-a. Also gives us: a = to (direction), hora = hour.

episodes.summary: Voy means 'I go' or 'I am going'. It's an irregular verb. Related to the English word 'voyage'.
episodes.notes: Spelled v-o-y. The v in Spanish sounds like a soft b. Related to 'vamos' (let's go / we are going).

episodes.summary: The letter v in Spanish is generally pronounced as a soft b (not a hard plosive b). In some regions like Argentina/Chile, you may hear more of a /v/ sound.
episodes.rule: V in Spanish = soft b (no burst of air). Hard b has a plosive burst.
episodes.notes: Test: hold paper in front of mouth — hard b moves paper, soft b doesn't.

episodes.summary: Just like English 'I am going to...', Spanish uses 'voy a + infinitive' to talk about the future. The 'a' is required because voy is a verb of movement.
episodes.rule: Voy a + infinitive = I am going to [verb]. The 'a' connects movement verbs to a following infinitive.

examples.english: I am going to confiscate it
I am going to examine it

episodes.summary: When a verb of movement (ir, venir, pasar, salir) is followed by another verb in the infinitive, they are connected with 'a'. Other verbs like querer, intentar, deber do NOT use 'a'.
episodes.rule: Verb of movement + a + infinitive. Non-movement verbs (querer, intentar, deber) connect directly without 'a'.

examples.english: I'm going to try to see them
I'm going to pass by to visit you
I want to pass by to show you something

episodes.notes: The 'a' appears after each verb of movement, even if there are other words in between.

episodes.summary: Continued practice of the 'a' connecting verbs of movement to following infinitives, even when other words intervene.

examples.spanish: Quiero saber si quiere venir a la casa a visitarme
examples.english: I want to know if she wants to come to the house to visit me

episodes.notes: Even with 'a la casa' in between, the 'a' before 'visitarme' is still required because of 'venir'.

episodes.summary: Los is the plural of lo. Lo = him/it, los = them. Just add -s to make it plural.
episodes.rule: lo → los (plural). Him/it → them.

examples.english: I am going to see them
I am trying to see them

episodes.summary: Ver means 'to see'. Related to English 'view'.

examples.english: I am going to see them

episodes.notes: Connected to English 'view'.

episodes.summary: Ver means to see/to view. It is slightly irregular in the 'I' form: veo (not vo). Other forms are regular: ve, ven, ves, vemos.
episodes.rule: ver: veo (I), ve (he/she), ven (they), ves (you informal), vemos (we). The 'I' form is veo, not vo.
episodes.notes: The 'I' form veo is slightly irregular — vo was too short, so it became veo.

name: Finding Latin equivalents for everyday English

episodes.summary: When you don't know a Spanish word, find the Latin/formal English equivalent. E.g., 'carry on' → 'continue' → continuation → continuación → continuar.

examples.english: I want to carry on / continue

episodes.summary: The 'to' form of intento is intentar. It's used like English 'to try' and does not require 'a' before a following infinitive.

Lo (masculine) becomes la (feminine) by changing o to a. La means 'her', a feminine 'it', or 'you (formal, female)'. Gender belongs to the word, not the object — two words for the same object can have different genders.
episodes.rule: Lo (him/it masculine) → La (her/it feminine). Words ending in -a are usually feminine.

examples.english: I want to see her / I want to see it (feminine)

episodes.notes: Gender is a property of the word, not the object itself.

episodes.summary: Quiere means 'he wants', 'she wants', 'it wants', or 'you (formal) want'. These four always group together in Spanish verb conjugation.
episodes.rule: He/she/it/you (formal) always share the same verb form.

examples.english: He/she wants to visit me / You (formal) want to visit me
Do you want to visit me?
Don't you want to visit me?

episodes.summary: In Spanish, questions are formed simply by using question intonation. No word order change or auxiliary verb ('do') is needed.
episodes.rule: Make a statement sound like a question with your voice. No 'do/does' equivalent needed.

examples.english: Do you want to visit me?
Don't you want to visit me?

episodes.summary: 'Por qué' means both 'why' and 'because'.

examples.english: Why don't you want to visit me?

examples.spanish: ¿Por qué no quiere venir a visitarme?
examples.english: Why don't you want to come to visit me?

episodes.summary: Venir means 'to come'. It's an -ir verb and a verb of movement, so it takes 'a' before a following infinitive. Related to 'conveniente' (convenient = 'with coming') and 'bienvenido' (welcome).
episodes.rule: Venir is a verb of movement: venir a + infinitive.

examples.english: Do you want to come to the house?
Do you want to come to visit me?

episodes.notes: Memory hooks: conveniente = 'with coming'; bienvenido = 'well come' (welcome).

episodes.summary: Debo means 'I owe' and is used like 'I must'. Connected to English 'debt' (which has a silent b from the same Latin root).
episodes.rule: Debo + infinitive = I must [verb]. Literally 'I owe to [verb]'.
episodes.notes: The English word 'debt' is spelled d-e-b-t; the silent b comes from 'debo'.

episodes.summary: 'La' is the feminine definite article (the). 'Casa' means house. 'A la casa' = to the house.

examples.english: Do you want to come to the house?

episodes.summary: Formal address uses lo/la and quiere for 'you'. Informal uses te. Usage varies hugely by dialect — Spain uses formal more; Buenos Aires almost never uses it sincerely.
episodes.rule: Formal you: lo (male), la (female). Informal you: te.
episodes.notes: In Buenos Aires, formal address is used more for sarcasm than sincerity. In Spain, formal is default with strangers.

episodes.summary: Saber means 'to know'. It's an -er verb. Related to English 'savvy' and 'Homo sapien'. The -o → -e pattern for he/she/it applies: quiero/quiere, so saber follows similar patterns.

examples.spanish: Quiero saber por qué no quiere saber
examples.english: I want to know why he doesn't want to know

episodes.notes: Memory hooks: 'savvy' in British slang, 'Homo sapien' = the wise creature.

episodes.summary: Sí means 'yes' and si (same word) also means 'if'.

examples.english: I want to know if you want to know
I want to know if he wants to invite me

episodes.summary: Explanation in Spanish is 'explicación', not 'explanación'. This is an exception. English also has this root: 'inexplicable'.
episodes.rule: Explanation = explicación (exception to normal pattern). To explain = explicar.

examples.english: I want to explain something to you

examples.spanish: No intento justificarme, pero quiero explicarte algo
examples.english: I'm not trying to justify myself, but I want to explain something to you

episodes.notes: English has both roots: 'explain' and 'inexplicable' — the c-sound root is the one Spanish uses.

episodes.summary: Complex sentences should be built block by block, giving 100% mental energy to each block then forgetting it. This makes seemingly impossible sentences achievable.

examples.spanish: Quiero saber por qué no quiere venir a la casa a visitarme
examples.english: I want to know why she doesn't want to come to the house to visit me

episodes.notes: Divide up piece by piece. Once you've said a block, you don't need to hold it.

episodes.summary: All Spanish infinitives ('to' forms) end in -ar, -er, or -ir. Verbs are identified by the ability to put 'to' in front. This ending is the key to conjugation.
episodes.rule: All infinitives end in -ar, -er, or -ir. We identify verbs by the 'to' form.

episodes.summary: To make the 'I' form in present tense, remove -ar/-er/-ir and add -o. The accent must move to the penultimate syllable. If the accent stays on the final syllable (e.g., intentó), it means the past tense (he tried).
episodes.rule: Remove -ar/-er/-ir, add -o. Stress penultimate syllable. Final stress = past tense (he/she).
episodes.notes: Intentar → intento (I try, penultimate stress). Intentó (final stress) = he tried. This distinction is critical.

episodes.summary: Comer means 'to eat'. It's an -er verb. Como = I eat.

examples.english: I eat tomorrow / I'm eating tomorrow

name: Present tense for future with context

episodes.summary: In Spanish, if there's a future context word (like 'mañana'), you can just use the present tense to express the future. This is perfectly natural in Spanish.
episodes.rule: Present tense + future context word (mañana, etc.) = future meaning.
episodes.notes: Sounds odd in English but is perfectly natural Spanish.

episodes.summary: Spanish can use the present tense to talk about the future when future context is provided (mañana, más tarde, esta noche, pronto, la semana que viene). This mirrors English usage like 'I'm seeing her tomorrow'.
episodes.rule: With future context words (mañana, pronto, la semana que viene, etc.), the present tense can express future meaning.

examples.spanish: Nos vemos la semana que viene
examples.english: We'll see each other next week

episodes.notes: 'Nos vemos' is a common way to say goodbye — the future context is implied by the farewell situation.

episodes.summary: Tener means 'to have'. The -tain in English verbs (contain, obtain, sustain, maintain, entertain) corresponds to tener. 'I have' is irregular: tengo (not teno).
episodes.rule: English -tain = Spanish tener. Contain = contener, obtain = obtener, sustain = sostener, maintain = mantener, entertain = entretener.

examples.english: I'm going to have it later

episodes.notes: Tener reflects a symbiotic relationship: when you own something, you also belong to it — you must maintain, sustain, contain it.

episodes.summary: Some verbs have an irregular 'I' form with -go instead of -o. Tener → tengo (not teno), venir → vengo (not veno). These group together — recognizing the pattern helps.
episodes.rule: Tener → tengo, venir → vengo. The -go irregularity appears only in the 'I' form.
episodes.notes: Irregular verbs group together in Spanish. Recognizing the pattern makes them manageable.

episodes.summary: For -ar verbs, the key vowel is /a/. Remove -ar, add -o for I. For he/she/it/you(formal), add -a. Add -an for they/you(plural). Add -as for you(informal). New verbs entering Spanish always become -ar verbs.
episodes.rule: -ar verbs: -o (I), -a (he/she/it/you formal), -an (they/you plural), -as (you informal). Key vowel = a.
episodes.notes: Any new verb entering Spanish becomes an -ar verb (e.g., tuitear, facebookear, googlear). The -er/-ir groups are historically closed.

name: Pronoun placement: before changed verb or after infinitive

episodes.summary: Pronouns (me, te, lo, la, los, las) go BEFORE a conjugated verb, or AFTER an infinitive (attached to it). When there's both a conjugated verb and an infinitive, you have the option of either position.
episodes.rule: Pronoun before conjugated verb: 'Lo quiero'. Pronoun after infinitive: 'Quiero verlo'. With only a conjugated verb, pronoun must go before.

examples.english: It's important to speak about it

episodes.notes: With a single conjugated verb and no infinitive, the pronoun must go before — no option.

episodes.summary: Hablar means 'to speak'. Spelled h-a-b-l-a-r with silent h. An -ar verb.
Durar means 'to last', derived from 'duration' (duración).

examples.english: It doesn't last a long time

episodes.summary: Masticar means 'to chew', from 'mastication'. Demonstrates that learning Spanish can reveal English vocabulary you didn't know.
episodes.notes: Learning Spanish can help you discover English words (e.g., mordent = biting, from morder = to bite).

episodes.summary: For -er/-ir verbs (a closed historic group), the key vowel is /e/. He/she/it/you(formal) gets -e, they/you(plural) gets -en, you(informal) gets -es. The 'I' form is still -o for all verb types.
episodes.rule: -er/-ir verbs: -o (I), -e (he/she/it/you formal), -en (they/you plural), -es (you informal). Key vowel = e.

examples.english: to contaminate → it contaminates (-ar key vowel a)

episodes.notes: Although there are three endings (-ar, -er, -ir), there are only two conjugation groups: -ar verbs and -er/-ir verbs.

episodes.summary: Words ending in -a are usually feminine (la casa). Words ending in -ión are always feminine (la opinión). Words ending in -idad are always feminine (la diversidad). Masculine words typically end in -o.
episodes.rule: Feminine endings: -a, -ión, -idad (all use 'la'). The gender belongs to the word, not the thing.

examples.english: the person (feminine word even for males)

episodes.notes: A man is 'una persona' — the word is feminine even when referring to a male.

episodes.summary: English words ending -ity become -idad in Spanish. All -idad words are feminine.
episodes.rule: -ity → -idad. All -idad words are feminine (la).
episodes.notes: From -idad words, you can guess adjectives by removing -idad and adding -o/-a: claridad → claro, generosidad → generoso.

episodes.summary: Adjectives ending in -o change to -a for feminine, and add -s for plural. -os (masculine plural), -as (feminine plural).
episodes.rule: -o (masc sing), -a (fem sing), -os (masc/mixed plural), -as (fem plural).

episodes.summary: When two object pronouns appear together (e.g., 'me' and 'lo'), the personal one (me, te, nos) comes first.
episodes.rule: Personal pronoun (me, te, nos) comes before thing pronoun (lo, la, los, las).

examples.english: They don't sell it to me
Why don't they sell it to me?
Why don't they sell it to us?

episodes.notes: Think about the verb first, then add the pronouns. Don't try to translate the whole package at once.

episodes.summary: Nos means 'us'. It follows the same placement rules as me, te, lo.
Claro means 'clear' as an adjective, but is also used conversationally like 'of course' to show agreement or understanding.
episodes.notes: Derived from claridad. Used a lot in conversation: 'claro, claro, claro'.

episodes.summary: Hacer means 'to do' or 'to make'. It's an -er verb with silent h. Conjugates regularly for he/she/it (hace), they (hacen), you informal (haces), but irregular for I (hago, covered later).

examples.english: What are you doing? (also: how are you?)

episodes.notes: ¿Qué haces? is a casual way to say 'how are you?' / 'what are you up to?'

episodes.summary: Across all verb types, -s marks informal you and -n marks they/you(plural). These associations persist throughout all Spanish verb tenses.
episodes.rule: -s = you (informal), -n = they/you (plural). This applies across all tenses.

episodes.summary: Puedo means 'I can' or 'I am able'. It's an -er verb. Puede = he/she/it can, puedes = you (informal) can. Related to English 'power'.
episodes.rule: Puedo (I can), puede (he/she can), puedes (you informal can). Like 'I am able to...' — takes infinitive directly, no 'a'.

examples.spanish: Quiero saber por qué no puedes venir
examples.english: I want to know why you can't come

episodes.notes: Related to 'power'. The d in puedo is quite soft in Spanish.

name: Always check the infinitive ending before conjugating

episodes.summary: Before conjugating any verb, take one split-second to identify: is it -ar, -er, or -ir? Otherwise your brain may use vowel harmony (putting an e because the stem has e sounds) and produce wrong forms.
episodes.rule: Always go back to the infinitive form before conjugating. Esperar is -ar, so he waits = espera (not espere).
episodes.notes: The brain naturally wants vowel harmony (espe- wants an -e ending). Turkish formalizes this as vowel harmony. Go back to the infinitive to fight this.

episodes.summary: Reinforced: must go back to the infinitive to avoid vowel harmony errors. With pensar, the student initially tried 'piense' (e matching ie) instead of 'piensa' (-ar verb requires -a).

examples.english: to think → he/she thinks (-ar verb, key vowel a)

episodes.notes: The brain wants vowel harmony (ie→e). Fight it by checking: pensar = -ar verb → key vowel is a.

episodes.summary: Esperar means both 'to wait' and 'to hope'. Related to English 'desperate' (des- = un-, so hopeless). No need for the word 'for' — just say 'they wait me'.
episodes.rule: Esperar doesn't need a preposition for its object: 'me esperan' = they wait for me.

examples.spanish: Quiero saber por qué no me esperan
examples.english: I want to know why they're not waiting for me

episodes.notes: Related to 'desperate': des- (un-) + esperar (hope) = hopeless.

episodes.summary: Salir means 'to leave' or 'to go out'. Irregular I form: salgo (same -go pattern as tengo, vengo). Regular for other forms. It's a verb of movement, takes 'a'.
episodes.rule: Salgo (I leave, irregular -go). Verb of movement: salgo a comer = I'm going out to eat.

episodes.summary: English words ending -ary become -ario in Spanish.
episodes.rule: -ary → -ario.
episodes.notes: Vocabulario comes from boca (mouth) — your 'mouth-ulary'.

episodes.summary: There's a difference between memorization (active, forced, borrowed) and remembering (natural, through practical application). The method avoids memorization but relies on internalization through constructing sentences.
episodes.notes: You borrow information when you memorize it. When you apply it practically in sentence construction, you internalize it — then it's yours.

episodes.summary: The 'I' form of saber is sé (not sabo). Very irregular — just two letters. The rest of saber's conjugation is regular: sabe (he/she knows), etc.
episodes.rule: Saber → sé (I know). Irregular only for 'I'. All other forms regular: sabe, saben, sabes.

examples.english: I don't know what he wants

episodes.notes: Lo sé is common because sé alone is very short. People often say 'lo sé' rather than just 'sé'.

episodes.summary: Él means 'he' (spelled e-l). Ella means 'she' (spelled e-l-l-a). Double l in Spanish is pronounced /y/. These are used for emphasis or clarification, not required.
episodes.rule: Él = he, ella = she. Use for emphasis or contrast. Not needed when the verb form is clear.

examples.english: I don't know what HE wants (emphatic)

examples.spanish: Yo no quiero invitarlo, pero ella sí
examples.english: I don't want to invite him, but she does

episodes.notes: Double l (ll) in Spanish = /y/ sound. Subject pronouns are used for contrast: 'I don't want to, but SHE does.'

episodes.summary: Double l in Spanish is pronounced /y/ (like 'yes').
episodes.rule: ll = /y/ sound.

episodes.summary: Adding 'que' to tengo changes 'I have' to 'I have to' (must). Que here has no comfortable direct translation. Tengo que is more everyday than debo (more serious).
episodes.rule: Tengo que + infinitive = I have to [verb]. Less serious than debo.
episodes.notes: Debo is more severe/serious (like English 'must'). Tengo que is more everyday (like 'have to').

episodes.summary: Poner means 'to put'. I put = pongo (irregular -go). English -pose verbs correspond to -poner in Spanish: compose = componer, suppose = suponer, impose = imponer, expose = exponer, oppose = oponer.
episodes.rule: English -pose = Spanish -poner. Poner → pongo (I put, irregular -go). Irregularity generalizes: suponer → supongo.

examples.english: I suppose so (literally: I suppose that yes)

episodes.notes: Supongo que sí = I suppose so. Que can mean 'that'.

episodes.summary: Review of verbs with irregular -go in the 'I' form: tengo, vengo, salgo, pongo. The -go only appears in the 'I' form; other forms are regular. The irregularity generalizes to compounds: obtener → obtengo, suponer → supongo.
episodes.rule: The -go irregularity only applies to the 'I' form. Compounds follow the same pattern.

episodes.summary: Cuándo means 'when'.

examples.english: When are you coming to eat?

episodes.summary: Tiene = he/she has. Tienen = they have. Tienes = you (informal) have. The irregularity (ie split) is explored more fully in lesson 15.

examples.english: I want to know what he/she has

episodes.summary: Recap of the key verbs that are followed by infinitives: voy a (going to), puedo (can), tengo que (have to), debo (must), quiero (want).

name: Stem vowel split: e → ie under stress

episodes.summary: When a verb has an 'e' in its stem (not the ending), and that e gets stressed in conjugation, it often splits into 'ie'. This explains querer→quiero, tener→tiene, venir→viene, pensar→piensa, entender→entiende, perder→pierdo. It doesn't split when something prevents it (like the -ng- in tengo/vengo).
episodes.rule: Stem 'e' splits to 'ie' when it receives the stress accent. Does NOT split in: the infinitive (stress on ending), or -go forms (the ng holds it). Splits in: I, he/she/it, you(informal), they forms.

examples.english: to want → I want (e splits to ie under stress)

episodes.notes: Visible between English and Spanish words too: tempo→tiempo, concert→concierto, November→noviembre. The -ng- in tengo/vengo 'holds up' the e and prevents the split.

episodes.summary: Pensar means 'to think'. It's an -ar verb with e→ie stem split. 'Pensar + infinitive' means 'to plan on doing something' (e.g., pienso comer = I plan on eating).
episodes.rule: Pensar + infinitive = to plan on [verb-ing].

episodes.summary: Pensar means to think, related to 'pensive'. It stem-changes e→ie. 'Pensar hacer' (to think to do) means to plan on doing.

examples.english: I think so (I think that yes)
What do you plan on doing?

episodes.notes: Related to English 'pensive'. 'Pienso que sí' = I think so (literally 'I think that yes').

episodes.summary: Entender means 'to understand'. An -er verb with e→ie split. More common than comprender (which is easier to remember from 'comprehend' and doesn't split).
episodes.notes: Comprender (from comprehend) also means to understand and doesn't have the e→ie split, but entender is more common.

episodes.summary: Perder means 'to lose'. An -er verb with e→ie split. Related to 'perish' and 'perishable'.
episodes.notes: Related to perish/perishable.

episodes.summary: Pasar can also mean 'to happen' (in addition to 'to pass'). ¿Qué pasa? = What's happening? ¿Qué pasó? = What happened? (past tense, with stressed final -ó).

examples.english: I want to know what's happening

episodes.notes: ¿Qué pasó? (stressed final -ó) is past tense = 'what happened?' — the stress distinction between present and past previewed earlier.

name: E→ie split visible between English and Spanish

episodes.summary: The same e→ie split can be observed between English and Spanish cognates: tempo→tiempo, concert→concierto, November→noviembre.
episodes.notes: These cross-language pairs help remember the pattern.

name: Stem change: e splits to ie under stress

episodes.summary: In the present tense, when the penultimate syllable containing 'e' is stressed, that 'e' can split into 'ie'. This happens because the accent on the penultimate syllable puts pressure on the vowel.
episodes.rule: When a verb has 'e' in its stem, that 'e' may split to 'ie' in present tense forms where it takes the stress (I, he/she, they, you informal).
episodes.notes: Always go back to the 'to' form (infinitive) before conjugating — this prevents guessing. The process: empezar → empieza, not empiezo → empieze.

episodes.summary: Practiced the e→ie split with querer and perder. Not all e's split — there's no way to know in advance, but most of the time it will happen.
Practiced with preferir and entender. The e splits in stressed forms (prefiero, entiende) but not in the 'we' form (preferimos, entendemos).

examples.spanish: Quiero saber por qué no entiende
examples.english: I want to know why he doesn't understand

name: Stem change: o splits to ue under stress

episodes.summary: In the present tense, when the penultimate syllable containing 'o' is stressed, that 'o' can split into 'ue'. This is shown both in verb conjugation and in cognates between English and Spanish.
episodes.rule: When a verb has 'o' in its stem, that 'o' may split to 'ue' in present tense forms where it takes the stress.
episodes.notes: This o→ue split can also be seen in English-Spanish cognates: port→puerto, force→fuerza, school→escuela, corp(orate)→cuerpo, new(nov-)→nuevo, mort→muerto/muerte, pueblo (population→población). These cross-language examples help internalize the pattern.

episodes.summary: Practiced with colgar (to hang). Not all o's split (comer → como, no split). There's no way to predict — you experiment and get feedback.
episodes.notes: Always go back to the infinitive to determine the correct ending — colgar is an -ar verb, so he/she form is cuelga, not cuelge.

name: Present tense accent on penultimate syllable

episodes.summary: Reinforced that the accent on the penultimate syllable in present tense is what causes the stem vowel to split.
Adding 'me' (myself) before a verb can change its meaning. 'Duermo' means 'I sleep' but 'me duermo' means 'I fall asleep' (I sleep myself).
episodes.rule: Adding 'me' (myself) before certain verbs changes the meaning, e.g., duermo = I sleep, me duermo = I fall asleep.

name: Verb forms can also be nouns

episodes.summary: Different versions of a verb — either the infinitive or a conjugated form — can serve as nouns when preceded by an article. Whatever you can put 'a' or 'the' in front of is a noun.
episodes.rule: If a verb form has an article (un/una/el/la) in front of it, it is being used as a noun.

examples.english: the power (noun, from poder = to be able)
an encounter (noun, from encontrar = to find)
a sample (noun, from mostrar = to show)

episodes.summary: To form the 'we' version of -ar verbs in present tense, remove -ar and add -amos. The accent falls on the -a- of -amos (penultimate syllable), which is in a different position than other forms.
episodes.rule: For -ar verbs: remove -ar, add -amos to get the 'we' form.

episodes.summary: The 'we' form does not trigger stem-vowel splitting because the stress moves to the ending (-amos), away from the stem vowel. The stem vowel is no longer stressed, so it stays intact.
episodes.rule: Stem vowels (e, o) do not split in the 'we' form because they are unstressed.
episodes.notes: The stem-changing issue is fundamentally about stress — only stressed stem vowels split.

episodes.summary: Spanish words beginning with al- typically come from Arabic, where al- means 'the'. Spanish has many more Arabic loanwords than English due to 800 years of Muslim presence in southern Spain. Sometimes the 'l' in al- assimilates to match the next consonant.
episodes.rule: Words beginning al- in Spanish are almost always from Arabic, where al- means 'the'.

examples.english: rice (Arabic: al-ruz, l assimilated to r)

episodes.notes: In Arabic, the 'l' of 'al' assimilates to match the next letter for about half the consonants. This is why azúcar (not alzucar) and arroz (not alroz). Spanish never has double-s (posible, pasión), but does have double-r (arroz, perro).

episodes.summary: Cerrar means to close. It stem-changes e→ie in stressed forms.

For -er verbs the 'we' form uses -emos, and for -ir verbs it uses -imos. This is the only difference between -er and -ir verbs in the entire Spanish language. For everything else they behave identically.
episodes.rule: Remove -er and add -emos (we speak: entendemos). Remove -ir and add -imos (we live: vivimos). This is the ONLY difference between -er and -ir verbs.
episodes.notes: The -ir 'i' appears in the 'we' ending for the first time in present tense. For all other conjugations, -er and -ir verbs behave identically.

episodes.summary: English speakers tend to harmonize vowels in Spanish words (e.g., saying 'veve' instead of 'vive'). The fix is to consciously remove the ending from the infinitive stem, see what's left, and add the correct ending.
episodes.rule: Always strip the ending from the infinitive to find the stem, then add the conjugation ending. Don't let vowel sounds bleed into adjacent syllables.

episodes.summary: An important skill in language learning is working backwards from a known conjugated form to derive other forms. From 'vamos' (we go), knowing -amos means it's an -ar verb pattern, you can strip to 'va' and build all other forms.
episodes.rule: From the 'we' form, identify the verb group vowel (-amos = a, -emos = e, -imos = i), strip the ending to get the base, then add other endings.
episodes.notes: Cross-language vowel splitting helps too: Italian sempre → Spanish siempre, French bon/bene → Spanish bueno/bien.

episodes.summary: Instead of only building up from verb roots, you can work backwards from a word you encounter. Seeing 'unidos' (united), identify -ido was added (so -ir or -er verb), remove it to find unir (to unite).
episodes.rule: See a participle or conjugated form -> identify what was added -> remove it to find the infinitive.
episodes.notes: When guessing between -ir and -er, the one you've heard before will feel more natural. This intuition grows with practice.

episodes.summary: The full present tense paradigm of ir assembled: voy, va, vas, van, vamos. Ir is very irregular because it historically merged from two different verbs. The infinitive 'ir' is used after other verbs (quiero ir, puedo ir, debo ir, tengo que ir).
episodes.rule: ir = to go. voy (I go), va (he/she/it/you formal go), vas (you informal go), van (they go), vamos (we go).

examples.english: Where are you going? (To where are you going?)

episodes.notes: Ir is actually two historical verbs merged together — the infinitive 'ir' comes from one verb, the conjugated forms (voy, va, etc.) from another. This explains the extreme irregularity.

episodes.summary: Words ending in -a are feminine, words with -ión or -idad are feminine, but words ending in -e have no predictable gender. You must look at surrounding words (articles, demonstratives) to determine gender.
episodes.rule: Nouns ending in -e can be either masculine or feminine. Use context clues (el/la, este/esta) to determine gender.

episodes.summary: The word for 'and' in Spanish is 'y', spelled as just the letter y.
episodes.rule: y = and

examples.spanish: No como carne y no tomo alcohol
examples.english: I don't eat meat and I don't drink alcohol
I eat meat and I drink alcohol

episodes.summary: Without future context, you can use 'ir a' (going to) + infinitive to express the future, just as in English. Voy a, vamos a, va a, vas a, van a.
episodes.rule: ir a + infinitive = going to (do something). Object pronouns attach to the infinitive or go before the conjugated verb.

examples.english: Are you going to see him soon?
Are you going to see us soon?
Are you going to see us soon? (alternate pronoun position)

examples.spanish: ¿Por qué no vas a vernos pronto?
examples.english: Why aren't you going to see us soon?

episodes.summary: Nos means us, ourselves, or each other. It follows the same positioning rules as other object pronouns (before changed verb or after infinitive).

examples.english: Are you going to see us soon?
Are you going to see us soon?

name: la semana que viene = next week

episodes.summary: 'Next week' is expressed as 'the week that comes' — la semana que viene. This provides future context allowing present tense for future meaning.
episodes.rule: la semana que viene = next week (the week that comes)

examples.spanish: Nos vemos la semana que viene

episodes.notes: The process of finding 'viene' is more important than memorizing it: venir → viene.

episodes.summary: 'Next week' in Spanish is expressed as 'the week that comes' - la semana que viene.

examples.spanish: Los compro la semana que viene.

name: Haber: auxiliary 'to have' for past tense

episodes.summary: Haber means 'to have' in the sense of 'I have eaten' (not possession, which is tener). It is extremely irregular. The forms can be remembered by the two vowels in haber: /e/ gives he (I have) and hemos (we have); /a/ gives ha (he/she/it/you formal has), has (you informal have), han (they have).
episodes.rule: haber vowels: e → he (I have), hemos (we have). a → ha (he/she/you formal have), has (you informal have), han (they have). I and we are connected (he→hemos). The rest build from ha: ha, has, han.
episodes.notes: Haber is spelled h-e: the silent h comes from haber. Only two verbs form 'I' this way: sé from saber, and he from haber. This 'have' past is used even more in Spanish than English — you can use it where English would use simple past.

episodes.summary: Quick review of haber forms using the two-vowel mnemonic: e → he, hemos; a → ha, has, han.
To form the past participle needed after haber, -ar verbs drop -ar and add -ado. This is much simpler than English where past participles are highly irregular (eaten, gone, wanted).
episodes.rule: For -ar verbs: remove -ar, add -ado to get the past participle (hablar → hablado, tomar → tomado).

examples.spanish: Lo hemos anticipado por mucho tiempo
examples.english: We have anticipated it for a long time

episodes.summary: With haber + past participle, there are two changed verbs together (he hablado), so object pronouns can only go before the first changed verb. You cannot attach them to the participle.
episodes.rule: With haber constructions, object pronouns go before haber: lo he terminado (not he lo terminado or he terminadolo).

episodes.summary: With haber + past participle, both are changed verbs, so object pronouns can only go before haber. There is no infinitive to attach them to.
episodes.rule: In haber constructions, the pronoun goes before haber: lo he terminado. After the infinitive or before the changed verb — here both verbs are changed, so only one position works.
episodes.notes: This is a rare situation with two changed verbs together. Usually after a changed verb comes a complete verb (quiero terminar).

episodes.summary: Por means 'for' when talking about periods of time. Also appears in 'por favor' (please, literally 'for a favour').
episodes.rule: por = for (time durations)

examples.spanish: Lo hemos anticipado por mucho tiempo
examples.english: We have anticipated it for a long time

episodes.summary: Tiempo means time. Related to the English musical term 'tempo'. The e in tempo splits to ie under stress, giving tiempo.
episodes.notes: English 'tempo' (used in music) → Spanish 'tiempo' (e→ie split)

episodes.summary: For -er and -ir verbs, the past participle is formed by removing -er/-ir and adding -ido. This is the same for both verb groups — the only difference between -er and -ir verbs remains the 'we' present tense form.
episodes.rule: For -er/-ir verbs: remove -er or -ir, add -ido (vender → vendido, venir → venido, perder → perdido).

examples.english: to come → come (as in bienvenido = well-come)

episodes.notes: Bienvenido (welcome) comes from bien + venido = well-come.

name: Subject pronouns: yo, tú, él, ella, usted, nosotros, ellos, ellas

episodes.summary: Subject pronouns are optional and used for emphasis or clarification. They go in a different position than object pronouns. Two separate sets of words: subject pronouns (yo, tú, él, ella, usted, nosotros/as, ellos/as) and object pronouns (me, te, lo, la, nos, los, las).
episodes.rule: Subject pronouns: yo (I), tú (you informal), él (he), ella (she), usted (you formal), nosotros/nosotras (we), ellos/ellas (they). These are optional, for emphasis/clarification. Object pronouns: me, te, lo, la, nos, los, las.
episodes.notes: Tú (subject, 'you see') vs te (object, 'I see you') — English doesn't distinguish these for 'you' but does for he/him, she/her. Nosotras is for an all-female group. Usted is a contraction of 'vuestra merced' (your honour). Ellos/ellas: double-l pronounced /y/ in most dialects.

name: tú vs te: subject vs object 'you'

episodes.summary: Tú is the subject pronoun (you see = tú ves) while te is the object pronoun (I see you = te veo). English doesn't change 'you' in these positions, which can cause confusion for learners.
episodes.rule: tú goes with yo, él, ella (subject position). te goes with me, lo, la (object position).

episodes.summary: A small group of nouns ending in -ma are masculine, not feminine. These words come from Greek, where they were neuter (which looks like masculine). When they entered Spanish via Latin, they were treated as masculine.
episodes.rule: Nouns ending in -ma are typically masculine (Greek origin): el problema, el sistema, el programa, el aroma, el panorama, el clima.
episodes.notes: A very common error is saying 'la problema'. These words come from Greek (problema=provlima, system=systima). Greek neuter words look like masculine and were adopted as masculine in Spanish.

episodes.summary: Sentir means to feel, related to 'sensation'. Can also mean to perceive/smell. Past participle is sentido.
episodes.notes: Related to English 'sensation'.

name: Reflexive verbs: perderse = to get lost

episodes.summary: To say 'I got lost' in Spanish, you say 'I have lost myself' (me he perdido). The reflexive pronouns me/te/nos go before haber. Combined with emphatic subject pronouns: yo me he perdido, tú te has perdido, nosotros nos hemos perdido.
episodes.rule: Reflexive pronoun + haber + past participle. The reflexive pronoun goes before haber.

examples.english: I have got lost (I have lost myself)

episodes.notes: When me he sounds together, the two /e/ sounds blend. Just as 'me preparo' = I get ready (I ready myself), 'me he perdido' = I got lost (I have lost myself).

episodes.summary: Dar means to give. The 'I' form is irregular: doy (ending in -oy, like voy and estoy). Other forms are regular: da, dan, das.
episodes.rule: dar: doy (I give), da (he/she/you formal give), dan (they give), das (you informal give).
episodes.notes: Doy ends in -oy like voy (I go).

episodes.summary: Dar is an -ar verb but takes -er/-ir endings in the preterite. It is the only verb that switches groups this way in the preterite.
episodes.rule: Dar in the preterite: di, dio, dimos. Note: 'dio' has no written accent (unlike regular -er/-ir preterite forms like comió).

name: Double object pronouns: person before thing

episodes.summary: When two object pronouns appear together (e.g., 'give me it'), the personal pronoun (me, te, nos) comes before the thing pronoun (lo, la, los, las). This order is: me/te/nos + lo/la/los/las + verb.
episodes.rule: Personal pronoun (me, te, nos) comes before thing pronoun (lo, la): me lo dan (they give me it), te lo doy (I give you it).

examples.english: They give it to me (they give me it)
I'll give it to you tomorrow
Are you (formal) giving it to me tomorrow?
Are YOU giving it to me tomorrow?
Are you guys giving it to us tomorrow?
Are YOU guys giving it to us tomorrow?
I want to give it to you

episodes.notes: In Spanish you say 'they give me it' not 'they give it to me' — no preposition needed.

episodes.summary: Ustedes is the plural of usted (you formal). Formed by adding -es to usted (since it ends in a consonant, you add -es not just -s). Ustedes takes the same verb form as ellos/ellas.
episodes.rule: Words ending in a consonant form plural with -es: usted → ustedes.

examples.english: Are you guys giving it to us tomorrow?

episodes.summary: Algo means something. Used in giving context.
In Spanish, me can mean me, to me, for me, or from me — context determines which. Spanish tends not to need prepositions like 'to', 'for', 'from' where English does. The same applies to te and nos.
episodes.rule: me/te/nos can mean the direct object OR indirect object — no preposition needed. 'He speaks to me' = me habla. 'He buys it for me' = me lo compra.

examples.english: They speak to me about it
Shall I buy it for you?
Do you want to do it for me?
Can you cook something for me?

episodes.notes: Shall I...? in English just becomes a question in Spanish: ¿Te lo compro? Context distinguishes 'for you' from 'from you'.

episodes.summary: Quedar means 'to remain' or 'to be left over'. To say 'I stay', you say 'I remain myself': me quedo. The reflexive is required for the meaning of 'stay'.
episodes.rule: quedar = to remain. me quedo = I stay (I remain myself). te quedas = you stay. nos quedamos = we stay.

examples.spanish: Me quedo aquí hasta las diez
examples.english: I'm staying here until ten o'clock
We are staying until nine o'clock

episodes.notes: The me/te/nos is not optional here — it's required to get the meaning of 'stay'. The emphatic pronoun (yo, tú, nosotros) is separate and optional.

episodes.summary: Extended practice combining quedarse with multiple verb constructions: voy a quedarme, quiero quedarme, debemos quedarnos, tengo que quedarme, puedo quedarme. Also practiced in present perfect: me he quedado, nos hemos quedado, te has quedado.

examples.spanish: Voy a quedarme hasta las siete
examples.english: I'm going to stay until seven o'clock
Do you plan on staying here tonight?

episodes.notes: The reflexive pronoun (me/te/nos) attaches to the infinitive (quedarme) or goes before the changed verb (me he quedado). It is not optional — it's required for the meaning of 'stay'.

episodes.summary: Reviewed quedarse with a complex sentence combining it with ir a and estar with porque.

examples.spanish: No vamos a quedarnos porque estamos muy cansados
examples.english: We're not going to stay because we're very tired

examples.spanish: No nos vamos a quedar porque estamos muy cansados
examples.english: We're not going to stay because we're very tired (alternate pronoun position)

episodes.summary: 'At X o'clock' is expressed as 'a las X' (literally 'at the X hours'). The 'horas' is implied and not said.
episodes.rule: a las + number = at X o'clock. (a las seis = at six o'clock, a las ocho = at eight o'clock)
episodes.notes: Literally 'at the six hours' (a las seis horas), but horas is dropped.

episodes.summary: Hasta means 'until'. Appears in the well-known phrase 'hasta la vista' (until the seeing/until we see each other).
episodes.rule: hasta = until

examples.spanish: Me quedo aquí hasta las diez

name: Present perfect used more broadly in Spanish

episodes.summary: The present perfect (he cocinado) can be used in Spanish where English would use simple past. 'I cooked at 6' can be 'he cocinado a las seis' in Spanish — it sounds natural even with specific time references.

examples.english: I cooked at six o'clock (I have cooked at six)

name: Object pronoun position: before changed verb or after infinitive

episodes.summary: Reinforced the precise rule: object pronouns go after the complete verb ('to' form / infinitive) or before the changed verb. Not 'at the beginning' — before the changed verb. This precision matters when sentences have nouns or other words before the verb.
episodes.rule: Object pronouns go: (1) after the infinitive (attached), or (2) before the changed/conjugated verb. Never simplify to 'at the beginning'.

examples.english: We haven't stayed (no before nos, nos before hemos)

episodes.notes: The teacher stresses: if you tell yourself 'at the beginning' instead of 'before the changed verb', you lose clarity when nouns enter the sentence.

name: Llamar = to call; me llamo = my name is

episodes.summary: Llamar means to call/to phone. 'Me llamo' (I call myself) is how you say 'my name is'. '¿Cómo te llamas?' (how do you call yourself?) = what is your name?
episodes.rule: me llamo = my name is (I call myself). ¿Cómo te llamas? = What is your name? (How do you call yourself?)

examples.english: What is your name? (How do you call yourself?)

episodes.notes: Double-l (ll) pronounced like /y/ in most Spanish-speaking regions.

episodes.summary: Cómo means 'how' in Spanish.

examples.english: What is your name? (How do you call yourself?)

name: Adjectives come after the noun in Spanish

episodes.summary: In Spanish, adjectives (describing words you can put 'is' in front of) come after the noun they describe. Instead of 'the global situation', you say 'the situation global'. There is some flexibility for emphasis, but the standard position is after.
episodes.rule: Adjectives follow the noun: la situación global (not la global situación). Adjectives also become plural when the noun is plural.

episodes.summary: The indefinite article 'a' is 'un' for masculine nouns and 'una' for feminine nouns.
episodes.rule: un = a (masculine). una = a (feminine). El/la = the, un/una = a.

examples.english: I want to buy a digital camera

episodes.summary: Reinforced that gender belongs to the noun/word, not the object. Different words for 'car' have different genders: coche (masculine), carro (masculine), máquina (feminine), auto (masculine).
episodes.notes: Gender is a property of the word, not the object — the same physical car can be el coche or la máquina.

episodes.summary: El becomes los in plural; la becomes las. Nouns and adjectives both take plural -s.

name: Modal verbs as shortcuts for conversation

episodes.summary: Using modal verbs (puedo, quiero, debo, tengo que) with infinitives is a 'shortcut' — even though the sentence is longer, it's easier to build because the modal is practiced frequently and the main verb stays in the infinitive. This lets you become conversational faster.
episodes.rule: puedo/quiero/debo/tengo que + infinitive is easier to build than direct conjugation. Use these shortcuts to get conversational, then notice how native speakers say it differently.

examples.english: I can't find it (shortcut — longer but easier)
I don't find it (more natural but harder to build)
Can you see the big car? (shortcut)
Do you see the big car? (more natural)

episodes.notes: Once you're communicative and correct, you'll start noticing native speakers use direct conjugation where you use modals, and your speech will adjust naturally.

episodes.summary: Es means is (he/she/it is, you formal are). Son means they are / you guys are. These come from ser, used for characteristics.
episodes.rule: es = is (singular). son = they are / you (plural) are.
episodes.notes: In Spanish you don't need 'a' before professions: es profesor (not es un profesor).

name: Adjectives agree in gender/number with nouns

episodes.summary: Adjectives ending in -o change to -a for feminine. Adjectives NOT ending in -o don't change for gender (e.g., global stays global). But nouns can change to feminine (profesor → profesora) to differentiate. Understanding word type (noun vs adjective) determines behavior.
episodes.rule: Adjectives: only change gender if they end in -o (caro→cara). Otherwise no change (global stays global). Nouns: can change regardless (profesor→profesora, doctor→doctora).
episodes.notes: This is why knowing word type matters — adjectives and nouns behave differently for gender.

episodes.summary: Adjectives ending in -o change to -a for feminine. Adjectives ending in -e (like libre) do not change for gender.
episodes.rule: Only adjectives ending in -o change for masculine/feminine. Adjectives ending in -e stay the same.

episodes.summary: Words ending in a consonant form their plural by adding -es, not just -s.
episodes.rule: Consonant ending → add -es for plural. Vowel ending → add -s.

episodes.summary: English words ending in -ive become -ivo (masculine singular/neuter) in Spanish. This follows standard gender/number agreement: -ivo, -iva, -ivos, -ivas.
episodes.rule: English -ive → Spanish -ivo (masculine). Changes to -iva (feminine), -ivos (masculine plural), -ivas (feminine plural).
episodes.notes: Neuter/generic uses masculine singular: 'es adictivo' (it's addictive, referring to a situation).

name: English double consonants simplify in Spanish

episodes.summary: Random double consonants in English (ff, ss, gg) become single consonants in Spanish. Only double-r (rr) and double-n (nn) cross over.
episodes.rule: English double f, s, g → single in Spanish. Only rr and nn remain doubled.

episodes.summary: Muy means 'very'. Spelled m-u-y, the y sounds like /i/.

name: Ser vs Estar: characteristics vs states

episodes.summary: Spanish has two verbs for 'to be': ser (for characteristics) and estar (for states). This is not about permanent vs temporary — being a student is temporary but uses ser (it's characteristic of you). Being tired uses estar even if you're always tired (it's a state). Spanish forces speakers to decide: is this a characteristic or a state?
episodes.rule: Ser = characteristics (profession, personality, identity). Estar = states (tired, location, emotional state). Do NOT simplify to permanent vs temporary.

examples.english: I am always tired (still estar — it's a state)

episodes.notes: Un ser = a being (noun). Un ser humano = a human being. The word 'state' is related to 'estar'. Even death (muerto) is a state in Spanish, not a characteristic. Simplifying to temporary/permanent works most of the time but leads to frustration when Spanish disagrees.

episodes.summary: Ser is for characteristics (what someone/something is like), estar is for states (how someone/something is doing). The question '¿Cómo está?' asks how someone is doing, while '¿Cómo es?' asks what someone is like.
episodes.rule: Use estar for states (how is he doing: ¿Cómo está?) and ser for characteristics (what is he like: ¿Cómo es?).

examples.english: How is he? (How is he doing?)
How is he? (What is he like?)

episodes.summary: Some adjectives change meaning depending on whether they are used with ser or estar. Aburrido with estar means 'bored' (state), while with ser it means 'boring' (characteristic). Some adjectives only go with one verb, and dialect can affect which verb is used.
episodes.rule: Adjectives can change meaning with ser vs estar. Aburrido: estar = bored, ser = boring.

examples.english: It is boring. / He is boring.

episodes.summary: More adjectives that change meaning with ser/estar: listo with estar means 'ready', with ser means 'smart'. Bueno with ser means 'good person/good at something', with estar (for people) means 'attractive/in good form'. Feliz (happy) can go with either ser or estar.
episodes.rule: Listo: estar = ready, ser = smart. Bueno (people): ser = good person, estar = attractive. Feliz works with both ser and estar.

examples.english: He is attractive / in good form.
I want to know how he is doing.
I want to know what he is like.

episodes.notes: Whether an adjective can flexibly go with ser or estar depends on the word itself, not just its meaning. When unsure, use the persona safety net. Ser/estar gives Spanish speakers expressive tools that English lacks.

episodes.summary: Spanish determines ser/estar based on whether the concept itself is a characteristic or state, not on the specific usage. But you can say 'estoy siendo + adjective' (I am being + adjective) to use a characteristic adjective in a temporary-state context, exactly like English 'I am being sincere'.
episodes.rule: For characteristic adjectives that go with ser, use 'estar + siendo + adjective' to express a temporary state of being that way. Siendo is the gerund of ser.
episodes.notes: Spanish decides ser/estar based on the concept itself, not the usage context. Sincerity and honesty are concepts that are characteristics, so they go with ser by default.

episodes.summary: Estar is the only verb in Spanish where present tense forms have the accent on the final syllable (not penultimate). The 'I' form estoy ends in -oy (like voy, doy). All other forms are regular but with final-syllable stress: está, están, estás. The 'we' form estamos has normal penultimate stress.
episodes.rule: estar: estoy (I am), está (he/she/it/you formal is), están (they are), estás (you informal are), estamos (we are). Only verb with final-syllable accent in present tense.
episodes.notes: Estoy ends in -oy like voy (I go) and doy (I give). The accent pattern (final syllable) is unique to estar in the entire present tense. The 'we' form estamos is the exception — it has normal penultimate stress because -amos adds two syllables.

episodes.summary: Full paradigm drill with subject pronouns: yo estoy, él/ella/usted está, ellos/ellas están, nosotros/nosotras estamos, tú estás. Combined with modal verbs and ir a.
Reviewed that ser is where es and son come from. A being = un ser. Used for characteristics like professions even if temporary.

Ser is used for characteristics of being, as opposed to estar which is used for states. Ser by itself means 'being' (un ser = a being). All five present-tense forms are taught: soy, es, son, somos, eres.
episodes.rule: Ser conjugation: soy (I), eres (you informal), es (he/she/it/you formal), somos (we), son (they/you guys). Only four verbs use -oy for I: voy, doy, estoy, soy.
episodes.notes: Eres is the most irregular-looking form. The phrase '¿De dónde eres?' can help remember it. Somos is derived from son by removing the -n and adding -mos.

name: Estar for location (where you are = a state)

episodes.summary: Location uses estar because where you are is a state, not a characteristic. '¿Dónde estás?' = where are you? 'Estoy aquí' = I am here.
episodes.rule: Where someone/something is located uses estar (it's a state).

examples.english: I don't know where we are

examples.spanish: Voy a estar aquí más tarde
examples.english: I'm going to be here later
Where are you going to be?

episodes.summary: How are you? uses estar because you're asking about someone's state. Informal: ¿cómo estás? Formal: ¿cómo está? Plural: ¿cómo están?
episodes.notes: Uses estar because asking about someone's current state.

episodes.summary: Deber (must/to owe) can express probability, just like English 'he must be tired' meaning 'he probably is tired'. Same usage in both languages.
episodes.rule: deber + infinitive can express probability: debe estar cansado = he must be tired (probability).

episodes.summary: Practiced tener que with estar to express 'have to be'. Reviewed that tener comes from -tain words (maintain, contain, sustain, entertain, obtain).

examples.english: Where do you (plural) have to be later?

episodes.notes: Tener is found in English -tain words: maintain=mantener, contain=contener, sustain=sostener, entertain=entretener, obtain=obtener.

episodes.summary: Porque means 'because'. Note: por qué (two words, with accent) = why; porque (one word) = because.
episodes.rule: porque (one word) = because. por qué (two words) = why.

examples.spanish: No vamos a quedarnos porque estamos muy cansados
examples.english: We're not going to stay because we're very tired

episodes.summary: Spanish cannot begin a word with s+consonant (st-, sp-, sc-, etc.), so an e- is added to the beginning. This explains the difference between English 'state' and Spanish 'estar', and applies to many cognates.
episodes.rule: When an English word begins with s+consonant, the Spanish cognate will have an e- prefix.
episodes.notes: The o-to-ue split also applies in escuela (school).

episodes.summary: Ustedes is the plural of usted but is not necessarily formal. In most of the Spanish-speaking world (outside parts of Spain), ustedes is simply how you say 'you guys' without any formal connotation.
episodes.rule: Ustedes uses the same verb form as ellos/ellas (son, están, etc.).
episodes.notes: Only in some parts of Spain is ustedes considered formal in the plural.

episodes.summary: De means 'from' or 'of' in Spanish. It appears as a building block in many English words (deliver = de + liver/freedom, decipher = de + cipher/zero). Used with ser for asking where someone is from.
episodes.rule: To ask where someone is from: ¿De dónde + ser? (from where is...?)
episodes.notes: Where someone is from is a characteristic (ser), but where someone is located is a state (estar).

name: Persona as safety net for ser/estar

episodes.summary: When unsure whether an adjective can be used with ser directly, you can say 'soy una persona + adjective' to safely express a characteristic. Since 'I am a person' is inherently characteristic, whatever adjective follows persona is safe with ser.
episodes.rule: Use 'soy/es una persona + adjective' when unsure if an adjective works directly with ser. Persona is always feminine regardless of speaker.
episodes.notes: Persona is feminine, so the adjective always takes feminine form (ocupada, not ocupado), even when the speaker is male.

episodes.summary: The word 'tan' means 'so' as an intensifier, as in 'I am so tired' or 'it is so boring'.
episodes.rule: Tan is placed before the adjective: estar/ser + tan + adjective.

episodes.summary: Tan means 'so' as in 'so tired' (tan cansado). Used in structures like 'tan... que...' (so... that...).

examples.spanish: Estábamos tan cansados que dormimos todo el día.
examples.english: We were so tired that we slept all day.

examples.spanish: Estábamos tan cansados cuando llegamos que dormimos todo el día.
examples.english: We were so tired when we arrived that we slept all day.

episodes.summary: To express what you are doing right now in this moment, use estar + gerund. For -ar verbs, replace -ar with -ando. For -er/-ir verbs, replace -er/-ir with -iendo. This form is ONLY used for actions happening right now, not for future references as English does with -ing.
episodes.rule: -ar verbs: remove -ar, add -ando (hablar -> hablando). -er/-ir verbs: remove -er/-ir, add -iendo (comer -> comiendo). Always use with estar. Object pronouns can go before estar or after the gerund.
episodes.notes: Do NOT use this for future: 'I'm preparing it later' = 'Lo preparo más tarde' (simple present), NOT 'estoy preparando'. The simple present tense already covers both 'I prepare' and 'I am preparing' in most contexts.

episodes.summary: Further practice of present progressive for right-now actions, and reinforcing that it must NOT be used for future references. Includes the gerund of ser: siendo.

examples.spanish: Los compro la semana que viene.

examples.english: I am buying them now. (right now)

episodes.notes: Resist the temptation of negative language transfer from English, which uses -ing for future.

episodes.summary: English overuses -ing to refer to the future (I'm doing it tomorrow), but Spanish uses the simple present tense with future context instead.
episodes.rule: For future actions, use simple present + future context (mañana, más tarde). Do not use estar + gerund for future.
episodes.notes: This is why verbs are given two translations: 'hablo' = 'I speak' AND 'I am speaking'.

episodes.summary: The future tense is formed by adding endings derived from haber to the full infinitive. The endings are: -é (I), -ás (you informal), -á (he/she/it/you formal), -emos (we), -án (they/you guys). The accent always falls on the ending, 'pushing the accent into the future'. Works the same for -ar, -er, and -ir verbs.
episodes.rule: Take the full infinitive + add haber-derived endings: -é, -ás, -á, -emos, -án. The accent is always on the ending. Think out the whole verb first before adding the ending (preparar + é = prepararé, not preparé).
episodes.notes: Common mistake: saying 'preparé' instead of 'prepararé' by not thinking out the whole verb first. The future tense endings match haber forms (he/hemos/ha/has/han). Ir is regular in the future tense despite being very irregular in the present.

episodes.summary: Practiced future tense with dar (to give). Clarified that the future tense in Spanish is a literal question about the future, not a polite request like English 'will you...?'
episodes.notes: 'Will you give it to me?' in Spanish is a literal future question, not a polite request. Use 'can you' for requests.

episodes.summary: Reviewed the future tense and its connection to haber. Practiced with bailar. Reiterated that Spanish has three ways to express future: present with context, ir a + infinitive, and the future tense (least common).

examples.english: We are going to dance later.

episodes.summary: Quick recap of haber forms using the two-vowel trick: /e/ gives he (I have) and hemos (we have); /a/ gives ha (he/she has), has (you informal have), han (they have).
episodes.notes: With haber structures, the object pronoun (lo, la, etc.) must come before haber. There is no option of placement.

episodes.summary: Reviewed as one of the ways to express future. You can use present tense with context, ir a + infinitive, or the future tense. The future tense is not used as often as the other two methods.

examples.english: I'm having breakfast with them tomorrow. (present)
I'm going to have breakfast with them. (ir a)

episodes.notes: Having future context doesn't prevent using ir a. Both structures can coexist with mañana.

episodes.summary: When lo/la/los/las mean 'to him', 'to her', 'to them', 'for him', etc. (indirect object), they change to le (singular) and les (plural). Me, te, and nos do NOT change for this meaning - they already include it. The personal pronoun always comes before the object pronoun in double-pronoun constructions.
episodes.rule: lo/la -> le (to him/her/you formal). los/las -> les (to them/you guys). Me/te/nos don't change. In double pronouns, personal comes first: me lo, te lo, nos lo, etc.

examples.english: Will you guys give it to me?
Will you guys give it (f) to me?
They will give it to you.
They will give it to us.
They will not give it to us.

episodes.notes: Le is ambiguous - it could be 'to him', 'to her', or 'to you (formal)'. Context clarifies.

episodes.summary: 'To realize' in Spanish is 'darse cuenta' - literally 'to give oneself account'. Realizar means to realize a project (bring to fruition), not to realize mentally. Cuenta means account.
episodes.rule: Me doy cuenta = I realize. Te das cuenta = you realize. Nos damos cuenta = we realize.
episodes.notes: ¿Te das cuenta? is used a lot conversationally, meaning 'Do you realize? Do you know what I mean?'

episodes.summary: Reviewed direct object pronouns with ver (to see). Lo = him/it(m)/you(formal m). La = her/it(f)/you(formal f). Los = them(m)/you guys(m). Las = them(f)/you guys(f).

The distinction between direct and indirect objects is about feeling, not about whether English uses 'to'. Ask: 'Am I being cooked? Or am I being cooked FOR?' Direct = the thing directly acted on. Indirect = the person benefiting/receiving. With me/te/nos the form doesn't change anyway.
episodes.rule: If the person is being acted on directly (I see him), use lo/la. If the person is the beneficiary/recipient (I cook him something = I cook FOR him), use le/les. Me/te/nos cover both meanings.
episodes.notes: When combining personal + object pronoun, the personal one comes first: nos las cocina.

episodes.summary: To express 'himself', 'herself', 'themselves', 'itself', or 'oneself', lo/la/los/las all become 'se'. This is the third-person reflexive. Me/te/nos already include their reflexive meanings (myself/yourself/ourselves). Se is also the dictionary form for reflexive verbs: quedarse, darse cuenta.
episodes.rule: lo/la/los/las -> se for himself/herself/itself/themselves/oneself. Se does not change for singular/plural. In dictionary, reflexive verbs appear as -se form (quedarse, darse).
episodes.notes: Se is easy to remember because it's the beginning of 'self'. In the dictionary, quedarse (to stay) is separate from quedar (to remain). The se/-me/-te/-nos attaches to infinitives as one word: quedarse, quedarme.

episodes.summary: Practiced full conjugation of quedarse (to stay) with all persons including the new third-person se. Also practiced with emphasis pronouns (yo me quedo, tú te quedas, etc.).
episodes.rule: When a reflexive verb has a different meaning from its non-reflexive form, it gets a separate dictionary entry. Quedar = to remain. Quedarse = to stay.
episodes.notes: Nosotras nos quedamos for all-female group. Me queda bien = it fits me well / it suits me well (different meaning of quedar).

episodes.summary: After prepositions (con, para, de, etc.), Spanish uses subject pronouns: con él, para ella, de nosotros, con ustedes. The exceptions are 'me' which becomes mí and 'you informal' which becomes ti after prepositions.
episodes.rule: After prepositions: use él/ella/ellos/ellas/nosotros/nosotras/usted/ustedes. Exceptions: me -> mí, te -> ti. So: para mí, de ti, con él, de nosotros.
episodes.notes: Prepositions show movement or position. 'Es de ustedes' and 'es para ustedes' use ser (characteristic of the object), but 'está con ustedes' uses estar (location = state).

episodes.summary: Le and les effectively replace prepositions like 'to', 'for', 'from' in everyday Spanish. Instead of 'compro para ellos' you say 'les compro'. This makes Spanish use fewer prepositions than English. The meaning (to/for/from) is understood from context.
episodes.rule: In everyday speech, use le/les instead of para él/de él etc. 'Me lo compra' can mean 'he buys it for me' OR 'he buys it from me' - context clarifies.

examples.english: I buy for them. (= compro para ellos)

episodes.notes: Spanish uses fewer prepositions than English. You'll naturally start shedding prepositions as you listen more to Spanish.

episodes.summary: Decir means to say or to tell, as distinct from hablar (to speak/talk). Decir is an -ir verb.
Reviewed hacer (to do/make). hace = he/she does. Distinguished from ha (haber). Practiced with 'hacer un favor'.
episodes.notes: Common confusion: ha (from haber) vs hace (from hacer).

episodes.summary: Spanish verbs often use different prepositions than their English equivalents. Rather than memorizing blindly, analyzing why the preposition differs can aid memory and reveal how language shapes perception. Key pairings: enamorarse DE (fall in love WITH), pensar EN (think ABOUT), soñar CON (dream ABOUT), depender DE (depend ON).

examples.english: I fall in love with you.
I fall in love with him.
It depends on if she wants to go.

episodes.notes: Enamorarse de: 'of' is more detached than 'with', possibly reflecting different cultural perceptions of love. Soñar con: 'with' implies the dreamed person participates, possibly an old belief. Pensar en: may come from Arabic influence during 800 years of contact.

episodes.summary: To fall in love is 'enamorarse de' - literally 'to enamor oneself of'. It is a reflexive verb found in the dictionary as enamorarse. Uses the preposition de, not con.
episodes.rule: Enamorarse de = to fall in love with. Me enamoro de = I fall in love with.

examples.english: I fall in love with you.
I am falling in love with him.

episodes.summary: 'With me' and 'with you (informal)' have special forms: conmigo and contigo, written as single words. This is an exception to the normal preposition + pronoun pattern (de mí, para ti are two words, but conmigo/contigo are one).
episodes.rule: con + mí = conmigo (with me). con + ti = contigo (with you informal). Written as one word.

examples.english: Do you want to come with me?
Why don't you want to come with me?
You have to come with me.

episodes.notes: The -go in conmigo comes from nowhere historically. All other prepositions use mí/ti as separate words.

episodes.summary: The word for 'yes' (sí) is also the word for 'if' in Spanish.

examples.english: It depends on if she wants to go.

episodes.summary: Soñar means to dream. The o splits to ue when stressed (sueño, sueña, sueñan, sueñas) but not when unstressed (soñamos). The ñ is the only letter in Spanish not in English, pronounced like ny.
episodes.rule: Soñar + con = to dream about. O splits to ue when stressed: sueño, sueña. Does not split in soñamos.
episodes.notes: The ñ (n with tilde) is pronounced ny, as in mañana. There is no way to predict which verbs will have the o-to-ue split; if it happens in one stressed form, it happens in all stressed forms.

episodes.summary: To depend is depender, an -er verb. In Spanish you say 'depende de' (depends of) rather than 'depends on'.
episodes.rule: depender de = to depend on. Uses de, not en or another preposition.

examples.english: It depends on if she wants to go.

episodes.notes: Common mistake: conjugating depender as an -ar verb. Always check the verb type first.

episodes.summary: The conditional (would) is formed by adding -ía to the full infinitive. Unlike other tenses, I and he/she/it/you(formal) share the same form (-ía). Other endings: -íamos (we), -ían (they/you guys), -ías (you informal). Like the future tense, the accent is pushed to the ending and all verb types (-ar, -er, -ir) use the same endings.
episodes.rule: Full infinitive + -ía (I/he/she/it/you formal), -ías (you informal), -íamos (we), -ían (they/you guys). The accent stays on -ía in all forms.

examples.english: I would eat, but I don't have time.

examples.spanish: Me imaginaría que no quiere ir.
examples.english: I would imagine that he doesn't want to go.

episodes.notes: Both futures (will and would) share: adding to full infinitive, stressing the ending, and grouping all verb types together.

episodes.summary: Practiced the conditional with hablar and gustar. Emphasized the importance of starting with the verb conjugation before adding pronouns, to stay in control of the sentence-building process.
Practiced conditional structures: could (podría), should (debería), would like (me gustaría), with various sentence constructions.

examples.english: Could you give me a hand?
You should give me a hand.
You guys should give me a hand.
You guys should give us a hand.

examples.spanish: Me gustaría tomar una foto, pero no tengo mi cámara.
examples.english: I would like to take a photo, but I don't have my camera.

episodes.summary: Spanish doesn't have a 'gets' construction (it gets contaminated). Instead, use 'se + verb' to express this passive meaning: 'se contamina' = it gets contaminated (literally 'it contaminates itself'). The subject isn't literally acting on itself; it's just the structure Spanish uses.
episodes.rule: Use 'se + verb' to express 'gets + past participle' in English. Se contamina = it gets contaminated. Se transformaría = it would get transformed.
episodes.notes: This does not mean the subject is literally acting on itself. It is just the Spanish structure for passive 'gets' constructions.

episodes.summary: Imaginar means to imagine. You will often hear 'me imagino' (I imagine myself) used interchangeably with 'imagino'. Adding the reflexive doesn't really change the meaning.

examples.spanish: Me imaginaría que no quiere ir.
examples.english: I would imagine that he doesn't want to go.

episodes.summary: Gustar means 'to be pleasing', not 'to like'. 'I like it' = 'it is pleasing to me' (me gusta). The verb conjugates to match what is liked (gusta for singular, gustan for plural), not who likes it. Common error: saying 'me gusto' which means 'I please myself'.
episodes.rule: Me gusta = I like it (it pleases me). Me gustan = I like them (they please me). Always start by conjugating the verb for what does the pleasing, then add who is pleased (me/te/le/nos/les).

examples.english: I would like to eat now.
We would like to eat now.
I would like to see you.

episodes.notes: Start with the verb, then add the pronoun. 'Nos gustaría' not 'nos gustaríamos' - the subject is 'it' (the thing that pleases), not 'we'. Me gusto = I please myself / I like myself.

episodes.summary: Extended gustar practice with third-person indirect objects: le gusta (he/she likes it), les gusta (they like it), les gustan (they like them). Emphasized translating as 'to be pleasing to' rather than 'to please' because it preserves the 'to' that maps to le/les.
episodes.rule: Store gustar as 'to be pleasing to' (not 'to please') so the 'to' maps naturally to le/les. Le gusta = it is pleasing to him/her. Les gustan = they are pleasing to them.

examples.english: We like it. (It is pleasing to us.)
He/she likes it. (It is pleasing to him/her.)

episodes.notes: Gustar uses le/les (not lo/la). Always start with the verb (gusta/gustan), then add the pronoun.

episodes.summary: Review of gustar in the preterite and imperfect, emphasizing the literal 'it pleased me' processing. The choice between 'no me gustó' (event) and 'no me gustaba' (ongoing) depends on the speaker's mental framing.

examples.english: I wasn't liking it. / I didn't like it (ongoing).

episodes.notes: 'Me gusté' means 'I liked myself' -- a common error to watch for.

episodes.summary: Practice of gustar with third-person subjects (le gusta for he/she/you formal likes it), emphasizing the 'mid road' literal translation of 'it is pleasing to him/her'.

examples.english: He/she likes it. (It is pleasing to him/her.)

episodes.summary: Common verbs like poder lose their -e- when adding conditional/future endings. Poder + ía = podería, but through contraction becomes podría. This gives 'could' (would be able). The same contraction applies to both will and would futures.
episodes.rule: Poder -> podr- (not poder-) + conditional/future endings. Podría = I/he/she could. Podrías = you (informal) could. Podríamos = we could. Podrían = they could.

examples.english: Could you come to my house?
Could you tell me if you want to come?

episodes.notes: The -e- of poder fell away through centuries of frequent use: podería -> podría.

episodes.summary: Haber contracts in the conditional just like poder: habería -> habría. This gives 'would have', which combines with past participles (-ado/-ido) to express 'would have done'. Habría serves I/he/she/it/you(formal). Other forms: habríamos (we), habrían (they), habrías (you informal).
