episodes.rule: Habría + past participle = would have done. Habría ido = would have gone. Habríamos comido = would have eaten. Build complex sentences block by block.

examples.english: We would have been able to go.
They would have been able to eat.
They would have come to eat.
I would have liked it. (It would have pleased me.)
We would have liked them. (They would have pleased us.)

episodes.notes: With gustar in conditional perfect, think in Spanglish: 'I would have liked them' = 'They would have pleased us' = 'Nos habrían gustado'. Always start by identifying who is doing the verb.

episodes.summary: Combining haber in the conditional (habría) with the past participle to form 'would have' structures. Habría is irregular - the e of haber falls out naturally.
episodes.rule: Habría + past participle (-ado/-ido). The e in habería naturally contracts to habría.

examples.english: I would have been able to go out
I wouldn't have been able to go out

episodes.notes: The contraction from habería to habría happens naturally through frequent use. Only a few verbs have this contraction.

episodes.summary: Reviewed habría + participle for 'would have'. The e in habería naturally falls out to form habría.
episodes.notes: The contraction habería -> habría happens so naturally that speakers do it without thinking.

episodes.summary: Habría + past participle = would have done. Habría is the conditional of haber (the e falls away from habería).
episodes.rule: Habría + past participle = would have (done). Habríamos = we would have.
episodes.notes: The e of haber falls away: habería → habría.

episodes.summary: 'Should' is expressed as 'would owe': deber (must/owe) in the conditional = debería. Unlike English where 'should' drops 'to' (I should eat, not I should to eat), Spanish keeps the infinitive after debería.
episodes.rule: Debería = I/he/she should. Deberías = you should. Deberíamos = we should. Deberían = they should. Always followed by an infinitive (deberíamos hablar, not deberíamos hablado).

examples.english: You should come to my house.

examples.spanish: Deberías venir a mi casa a comer.
examples.english: You should come to my house to eat.

episodes.notes: In English, after can/should/must you don't say 'to' (I should eat). In Spanish, you still use the infinitive (deberíamos hablar). Using 'con él/con ella' instead of 'le' removes the ambiguity of le (him/her/you formal).

episodes.summary: Haber loses its -e- in the conditional, just like poder: habería -> habría. This is the same contraction pattern.
episodes.rule: Haber -> habr- + conditional endings. Habría, habrías, habríamos, habrían.

episodes.summary: Many Spanish words ending in -ma entered through Greek. In Greek they are neuter but look masculine, so they became masculine in Spanish despite ending in -a. Examples: el problema, el sistema, el paradigma, el esquema, el diagrama, el planeta.
episodes.rule: Words ending in -ma that came from Greek are masculine: el problema, el sistema, el diagrama, el esquema, el paradigma, el planeta.
episodes.notes: El esquema follows the e-prosthesis rule (scheme -> esquema, since Spanish can't start with s+consonant).

episodes.summary: Este (masculine) and esta (feminine) mean 'this'. Remove the t to get ese/esa meaning 'that'. Plurals: estos/estas (these), esos/esas (those).
episodes.rule: This: este (m), esta (f). That: ese (m), esa (f). Plurals add -s: estos, estas, esos, esas. Remove t from este/esta to get ese/esa.

examples.spanish: No quiero ese auto, porque es muy grande.
examples.english: I don't want that car, because it's very big.

name: La mano - feminine word ending in -o

episodes.summary: Mano (hand) is the only true feminine word ending in -o. La foto and la moto are not true exceptions - they are abbreviations of la fotografía and la motocicleta, retaining the feminine article.
episodes.rule: La mano = the hand. Feminine despite ending in -o. La foto = la fotografía. La moto = la motocicleta.
episodes.notes: Mano appears in English words: manuscript, manual, maintain (mantener), manage (manejar). Manejar means to handle/manage and also 'to drive' in some Latin American countries.

episodes.summary: Tomar means to take. Used for 'tomar una foto' (to take a photo). Sacar can also be used for photos.

examples.english: I would like to take a photo.

episodes.notes: Sacar can also be used for taking photos: estoy sacando una foto.

episodes.summary: Interesar works like gustar: translate as 'to be interesting to' rather than 'to interest'. Uses le/les for third person. Can be used with conditional: te interesaría (would it interest you).
episodes.rule: Me interesa = it interests me. Le interesa = it interests him/her. Les interesa = it interests them. Uses le/les, not lo/la.

examples.english: Is she interested in dancing tonight?

examples.spanish: ¿Le interesa salir a bailar esta noche?
examples.english: Is she interested in going out to dance tonight?
I would be interested in seeing it.

episodes.notes: Interesante (interesting) is the adjective. Interesar is the verb. Always check verb type before conjugating (-ar, -er, -ir).

episodes.summary: Parecer (to seem) works like gustar and interesar with le/les. 'Me parece' = it seems to me. 'Me parece que...' = it seems to me that... The 'que' (that) is obligatory in Spanish even though it's optional in English.
episodes.rule: Me parece = it seems to me. Me parece que + clause (que is obligatory). Me parece bien = it seems good/fine to me. Parecer is an -er verb.

examples.spanish: Me parece que no quiere salir esta noche.
examples.english: It seems to me that he doesn't want to go out tonight.

examples.spanish: Me parece que no le gusta.
examples.english: It seems to me that he doesn't like it.
It doesn't seem good to me.
It doesn't seem good to him/her.
Doesn't it seem ok to you (formal)?

episodes.notes: 'Me parece bien' uses bien (well) not bueno (good). There is a gray area between bien and bueno that you pick up through listening. The 'que' after parecer is obligatory in Spanish, unlike English where 'that' is optional. Spanish shows the that/what connection: que = that, qué = what, like English there/where, then/when.

episodes.summary: Spanish shows clearly that 'that' and 'what' are the same word (que/qué), just as English shows: that/what, there/where, then/when (remove t, add w).
episodes.rule: que = that (conjunction). qué = what (question). Same word, different function.

examples.spanish: Me parece que no quiere ir.
examples.english: It seems to me that he doesn't want to go.

episodes.notes: English parallel: that->what, there->where, then->when (replace t with w).

episodes.summary: Poner means to put. When English has -pose inside a verb, Spanish has -poner: compose=componer, suppose=suponer, oppose=oponer, impose=imponer. The com- in componer is actually con- (with), where n changes to m before p.
episodes.rule: English -pose = Spanish -poner. compose=componer, suppose=suponer, oppose=oponer, impose=imponer.

examples.english: I want to put it here.
I would like to put it here.

episodes.notes: Com- in componer is actually con- (with), where the n assimilates to m before p.

episodes.summary: Le can replace 'on it' or 'to it' when talking about putting something on something. 'Ponerle sal' = to put salt on it. Les for plural.
episodes.rule: Le replaces 'on it' with poner: ponerle sal = put salt on it. Ponerles sal = put salt on them.

examples.english: I want to put salt on it.
I would like to put salt on it.

examples.spanish: Me gustaría ponerle un poco más sal.
examples.english: I would like to put a little more salt on it.

examples.spanish: Me gustaría ponerles un poco más sal.
examples.english: I would like to put a little more salt on them.

episodes.summary: Un poco means 'a little'. Can be combined with más (more): un poco más = a little more.
Reviewed the present perfect formed with haber + past participle (-ado for -ar verbs, -ido for -er/-ir verbs). The past participle never changes regardless of subject.
episodes.rule: Haber conjugated + -ado (for -ar verbs) or -ido (for -er/-ir verbs). The participle is invariable after haber.

examples.english: I have been able to eat
I haven't been able to eat

episodes.notes: The participle after haber never changes - he bailado, han bailado, hemos bailado all use bailado.

episodes.summary: Introduced the imperfect tense as the 'line in the past' representing ongoing, habitual, or repeated past actions (I was doing, I used to do). For -ar verbs, remove -ar and add -aba as the base ending.
episodes.rule: For -ar verbs: remove -ar, add -aba (I/he/she/it/usted), -abas (tú), -ábamos (we), -aban (they/ustedes). The accent stays on -aba throughout.

examples.english: I was speaking / I used to speak
I was waiting / I used to wait
We were waiting for you (formal, masc)
They were trying to find it for me

episodes.notes: Three pasts in Spanish: (1) present perfect (he comido), (2) imperfect/line (comía), (3) preterite/dot (comí). The -aba form covers I/he/she/it/usted - same form for all.

episodes.summary: Practiced the imperfect -aba endings with new verbs cenar and quemar.

examples.english: I was having dinner / He was having dinner

episodes.summary: Practiced imperfect with tener (tenía) and combined with tener que for 'had to'. Even though English uses 'had to' (not 'was having to'), the imperfect works fine in Spanish.

examples.english: I used to have a big house
We used to have a big house

examples.spanish: Tenía cuatro, pero ahora no me queda
examples.english: I used to have four, but now I don't have any left
I had to see you yesterday
Didn't you have to do it yesterday?

episodes.notes: The accent stays in the same place on -ía: tenía, teníamos.

episodes.summary: The imperfect (line in the past) is used for ongoing states rather than point-in-time events. Spanish speakers saying 'they were speaking Spanish' use the imperfect because being a Spanish speaker is an ongoing characteristic.
episodes.rule: Use the imperfect for ongoing characteristics, not the preterite, even when English uses simple past.

examples.spanish: Había dos personas aquí que hablaban español
examples.english: There were two people here that spoke Spanish

episodes.notes: Don't translate directly from English 'spoke' — look at the context to decide line vs point.

episodes.summary: Introduced the framework of three pasts in Spanish: the 'I have eaten' past (present perfect), the 'line in the past' (imperfect - ongoing/habitual), and the 'dot in the past' (preterite - completed action).
episodes.rule: Line = I was eating, I used to eat, I would eat (habitual). Dot = I ate (completed). Have = I have eaten.
episodes.notes: All English past expressions fit into one of these three Spanish forms.

episodes.summary: When a verb's action isn't directed at something else, Spanish uses 'se' (itself) to show the subject is affected by its own action. This avoids leaving the sentence feeling incomplete. 'La cena quemaba' feels incomplete (what was the dinner burning?), but 'La cena se quemaba' means the dinner was burning/getting burnt.
episodes.rule: Use se + verb when the subject undergoes its own action (it breaks = se rompe, it burns = se quema). Without se, listeners expect to hear what the action is directed at.

examples.english: I get burnt in the sun

examples.spanish: Esos se rompen todo el tiempo
examples.english: Those ones break all the time

episodes.notes: Word order is flexible: La cena se quemaba = Se quemaba la cena. Without se, the verb expects an object.

episodes.summary: Taught este (this) and estos (these) for masculine nouns, showing the logic: este contains the /e/ of el, estos contains the /os/ of los. That/those formed by removing the t: ese/esos.
episodes.rule: This: este (masc sing), estos (masc plural). That: ese (masc sing), esos (masc plural). Mnemonic: este has /e/ of el, estos has /os/ of los. Remove t for that/those.
episodes.notes: The pattern este/estos maps to el/los. Remove t to get ese/esos (that/those).

episodes.summary: Reviewed este/estos with the mnemonic: este has /e/ of el, estos has /os/ of los.
For -er/-ir verbs in the imperfect, remove the ending and add -ía. This is the same sound as the conditional, but the conditional adds onto the full verb while the imperfect removes the ending first.
episodes.rule: Remove -er/-ir, add -ía (I/he/she/it/usted), -ías (tú), -íamos (we), -ían (they). Key contrast: comería (I would eat) vs comía (I was eating).

examples.english: I was eating / He was eating

episodes.notes: Conditional adds -ía to full verb (comería). Imperfect removes ending first (comía). Same -ía sound, different base.

episodes.summary: Se vende literally means 'it sells itself' and is used to mean 'for sale'. This is seen on houses and cars in Spanish-speaking countries.
episodes.notes: You will see 'Se vende' on houses and cars in Spanish-speaking places.

episodes.summary: How do you say? in Spanish is literally 'how does it say itself?' - another creative use of the reflexive se structure.

examples.english: How do you say? (How does it say itself?)

episodes.notes: Don't say '¿Cómo dices?' for this meaning - that means 'how do you (individually) say it?'

episodes.summary: Introduced present tense conjugation of decir. Irregular in he/she form (dice) but the we form escapes the irregularity.
episodes.rule: dice (he/she/it), dices (tú), dicen (they), decimos (we - regular pattern from decir)
episodes.notes: The we form tends to escape irregularity as the accent falls somewhere else.

episodes.summary: Quedar means to remain/be left over. Quedarse (reflexive) means to stay. 'Me queda uno' = I have one left (it remains to me one). 'Me quedo' = I stay (I remain myself).
episodes.rule: Quedar + indirect object = to have left (me queda uno). Quedarse (reflexive) = to stay (me quedo).
episodes.notes: The verb changes to plural when what remains is plural: me queda uno, me quedan dos.

episodes.summary: Se habla español means 'Spanish is spoken' or 'Spanish spoken' - literally 'Spanish speaks itself'. Another creative application of the reflexive se.
Reviewed llamar (to call) and the reflexive structure me llamo (I call myself) for introducing oneself, plus ¿Cómo te llamas? and ¿Cómo se llama?
episodes.notes: Double l (ll) creates a /yuh/ sound in most Spanish dialects.

episodes.summary: Reviewed how English -tain verbs convert to -tener in Spanish: contain=contener, obtain=obtener, maintain=mantener, sustain=sostener.
episodes.rule: English -tain becomes -tener in Spanish
episodes.notes: Mantener: man- is from mano (hand). Sostener changed slightly from sus- to sos-.

episodes.summary: Ver is irregular in the imperfect: veía (not vía). The same extra e irregularity from the present (veo) carries into the imperfect.
episodes.rule: veía (I/he/she), veías (tú), veíamos (we), veían (they)

examples.english: I was seeing / I used to see

episodes.notes: Same extra e as in veo (present). Only three verbs are irregular in the imperfect.

episodes.summary: Ser in the imperfect uses era as the base. Easy to remember because 'an era' in English is a line in time in the past.
episodes.rule: era (I/he/she), eras (tú), éramos (we), eran (they)

examples.english: I used to be a student

episodes.notes: Mnemonic: 'era' in English means a line in time in the past, just like the imperfect. No word for 'a' needed before professions: Era estudiante.

episodes.summary: Ir in the imperfect is iba - a blend of the -aba and -ía patterns. Very useful for 'was going to' + infinitive structures.
episodes.rule: iba (I/he/she), ibas (tú), íbamos (we), iban (they)

examples.english: I was going / He was going
I was going to call you
I was going to do it
We were going to make them

episodes.notes: Iba blends -aba and -ía. Don't forget the 'a' connector: iba A hacerlo (ir is an 'a' verb).

episodes.summary: Detailed exploration of the two words for 'for'. Para = for somebody, directed to someone. Por = because of, for time durations, for exchanges. Por qué (why) literally means 'because of what'. Porque (because) means 'because of that'.
episodes.rule: Para = for someone/something (destination). Por = because of, for time periods, in multiplication. Por qué = why (because of what). Porque = because.

examples.spanish: Por esta razón, no quiero ir
examples.english: Because of this reason, I don't want to go
I'm buying it for you (destined for you)
I'm buying it because of you

episodes.notes: Lo he comprado por ti = I bought it because of you (like an ashtray for a friend who smokes). Lo he comprado para ti = I bought it for you (as a gift, they'll take it home).

episodes.summary: Further contrasted por and para with estar: estoy para ti (I'm there for you, supportive), estoy por ti (I'm here because of you). Also por for exchanges: cambiar este por ese.
episodes.rule: Para = for someone (supportive/destination). Por = because of, for exchanges. Por is also used in multiplication (ocho por ocho).

examples.english: I have come because of you

examples.spanish: Es por ti que hemos venido
examples.english: It is because of you that we have come
I would change this one for that one
I would change these for those
I would change these for those (feminine)

episodes.notes: Por is written as 'x' in casual online Spanish (gracias x venir). Por is also used for times tables (8 x 8 = ocho por ocho).

episodes.summary: Por can mean 'by' as in 'by phone' (por teléfono), not just 'for'.
When talking about periods of time (for two weeks, for three days), use por, not para.
episodes.rule: Use por for time durations: por tres días (for three days)

examples.english: I will stay for three days
I would stay for three days

examples.spanish: Me quedaría por tres días, pero tengo que trabajar
examples.english: I would stay for three days, but I have to work

episodes.summary: After prepositions (para, por, de, en, con, a, sin), Spanish uses the infinitive (to form), NOT the -ing form as in English. 'Thanks for coming' = Gracias por venir, not *gracias por viniendo.
episodes.rule: After prepositions, always use the infinitive. Never use -ando/-iendo after a preposition.
episodes.notes: This is a key negative language transfer point. Spanish speakers learning English often say 'for see you' instead of 'for seeing you' - that mistake reveals the Spanish structure.

episodes.summary: Words that look similar between English and Spanish but differ in meaning. Discutir means to argue (not discuss). Actual means current (not actual). Actualmente means currently (not actually).
episodes.notes: Most Latin-English to Spanish vocabulary is reliable, but watch out for these false friends.

episodes.summary: Way/manner is forma in Spanish. 'A way of doing it' = una forma de hacerlo. 'In this way' = de esta forma.

examples.english: It's a way of doing it

examples.spanish: No creo en discutir de esta forma
examples.english: I don't believe in arguing in this way

episodes.summary: Whenever English 'to' means 'in order to', it must be translated as para in Spanish. The bare infinitive is not enough to convey this meaning.
episodes.rule: When 'to' means 'in order to', use para + infinitive. The infinitive alone does not carry this meaning.

examples.english: We have spoken in order to resolve it
We have spoken in order to try to resolve it
I'm here to see you (in order to)
I have called you to speak about it

episodes.notes: Spanish speakers learning English often say 'I am here for see you' - this mistake reveals the para structure. Negative language transfer can teach us the Spanish pattern.

episodes.summary: Reviewed estar + -ando (for -ar verbs) / -iendo (for -er/-ir verbs) for expressing right now actions. Can also be used with imperfect estar (estaba) for past progressive. Both estaba comiendo and comía mean 'I was eating' and are mostly interchangeable.
episodes.rule: -ar verbs: -ando. -er/-ir verbs: -iendo. Estoy comiendo = I am eating (right now). Estaba comiendo = I was eating.

examples.spanish: Estoy tomando un curso de español
examples.english: I am taking a Spanish course

examples.spanish: Estaba tomando un curso de español
examples.english: I was taking a Spanish course

episodes.notes: Object pronouns can attach to the gerund (explicándolo) or go before estar (lo estoy explicando). Estaba comiendo and comía are mostly interchangeable.

episodes.summary: The -ndo form (gerund) is appropriate in structures like 'he arrived wanting to speak with me', where English also uses -ing.

examples.spanish: Llegó a mi casa otra vez queriendo hablar conmigo.
examples.english: He arrived at my house again wanting to speak with me.

episodes.notes: Spanish uses -ing much less than English, but this is one situation where it is used.

episodes.summary: When English uses -ing as the subject of a sentence (Swimming is healthy), Spanish uses the infinitive instead.
episodes.rule: Use the infinitive, not -ando/-iendo, as the subject of a sentence.
episodes.notes: Spanish does not use -ing as much as English: not after prepositions, not as subject, not as adjective.

episodes.summary: When English makes an adjective from a verb using -ing (interesting, irritating), Spanish uses -ante for -ar verbs and -iente for -er/-ir verbs.
episodes.rule: -ar verbs: remove -ar, add -ante. -er/-ir verbs: remove ending, add -iente.
episodes.notes: This -ante/-iente pattern even exists in English: salient (from salir). A living being = un ser viviente.

episodes.summary: English 'could' has two meanings: past could (I couldn't do it yesterday = podía) and future/hypothetical could (I could do it tomorrow = podría). Past could uses the imperfect of poder.
episodes.rule: Past could = podía (imperfect). Future/hypothetical could = podría (conditional).

examples.spanish: No podía hacerlo porque no tenía el tiempo
examples.english: I couldn't do it because I didn't have the time

episodes.notes: With querer and poder, the imperfect works even when English doesn't use 'was wanting/was able' because wanting and being able are naturally ongoing states.

episodes.summary: Formed with había (imperfect of haber) + past participle. Always uses the imperfect (line) form of haber, never the preterite. Usage mirrors English 'had eaten', 'had gone'.
episodes.rule: Había + -ado/-ido. Always use imperfect of haber for 'had'. Usage is the same as English had + past participle.

examples.spanish: Él quería cenar conmigo pero yo ya había cenado
examples.english: He wanted to dine with me but I had already dined

examples.spanish: Lo buscaba porque yo lo había perdido
examples.english: He was looking for it because I had lost it

episodes.notes: Ya (already) tends to go at the beginning of its clause. Use pronouns (él, yo) when contrasting two people to avoid ambiguity.

episodes.summary: The -ado/-ido form used after haber can also be used as an adjective. After haber it never changes, but as an adjective it agrees in gender and number. These adjectives almost always go with estar, not ser.
episodes.rule: Participle after haber: invariable (lo he cerrado, la he cerrado). As adjective: agrees in gender/number (cerrado/cerrada/cerrados/cerradas). Almost always uses estar.

examples.english: I have closed it (invariable after haber)
I have closed it (feminine, still cerrado after haber)
It is closed (the door - feminine adjective)

episodes.notes: After haber: always invariable. As adjective: agrees with noun. Almost always with estar, with a couple of exceptions.

episodes.summary: Continued practice using past participles as adjectives with estar. Some like complicado can go with either ser or estar.
episodes.notes: Complicado can go with either ser or estar. Most participle adjectives tend toward estar.

name: Ser vs estar with participle adjectives

episodes.summary: Participle adjectives normally go with estar. But when saying 'it is a [noun]' with ser, the adjective is secondary to the noun classification. 'La silla está mojada' (the chair is wet) vs 'Es una silla mojada' (it is a wet chair).
episodes.rule: Use estar when the adjective is the main point (la silla está mojada). Use ser when classifying with a noun (es una silla mojada). Es una persona cerrada is fine because ser goes with the noun persona.

examples.english: He is closed to the idea (estar)
He/she is a closed person (ser + noun)
The chair is wet (estar for state)
It is a wet chair (ser for classification)

episodes.notes: When you say 'es un/una [noun]', ser pairs with the noun, not the adjective. The adjective is secondary.

episodes.summary: Cansado (tired) is actually the past participle of cansar (to tire). Me canso = I get tired. Me he cansado = I've gotten tired/fed up.

examples.english: I get tired / I get fed up
I've gotten tired / I've had enough

episodes.summary: Should = debería (conditional of deber, to owe/must). Should have = debería haber + participle.
episodes.rule: Debería + haber + past participle = should have done
episodes.notes: Lo can go before debería or attached to haber (haberlo).

episodes.summary: Could have = podría haber + participle. Uses the conditional of poder.
episodes.rule: Podría + haber + past participle = could have done

episodes.summary: Several common verbs have irregular past participles: romper->roto, morir->muerto, abrir->abierto, decir->dicho, hacer->hecho. These irregular forms are used both after haber and as adjectives.
episodes.rule: Romper->roto, morir->muerto, abrir->abierto, decir->dicho, hacer->hecho
episodes.notes: Dicho and hecho both have the /ch/ sound. Un dicho = a saying (noun). Un hecho = a fact (noun). Hecho en España = made in Spain. Muerto always goes with estar, never ser.

name: F to silent H sound change

episodes.summary: A phonetic change in Spanish history turned many f's into silent h's. This creates connections: hecho/fact, hablar/fable, huir/fugitive, hermano/fraternal, hembra/feminine, hierro/ferrocarril, hondo/profundo, humo/fumar.
episodes.rule: Latin f often became silent h in Spanish. Look for f/h pairs between English and Spanish.
episodes.notes: Also w/g pairs: war/guerra, William/Guillermo. These letter-pair connections aid vocabulary memory.

episodes.summary: Unlike English, Spanish never puts prepositions at the end of a sentence. 'Where are you from?' becomes 'From where are you?' (¿De dónde eres?). 'Who I work with' becomes 'with whom I work' (con que trabajo).
episodes.rule: Prepositions must come before their object in Spanish, never at the end of the sentence.

examples.english: Where are you from? (From where are you?)
Where are you going? (To where are you going?)

examples.spanish: No sé con cual quiere hacerlo
examples.english: I don't know which one he wants to do it with

episodes.notes: This doesn't sound formal in Spanish as it might in English - it's simply the only correct option.

episodes.summary: For relative clauses (the girl I work with), Spanish uses: con que, con cual, con la cual, con la que, con quien - all are correct. Que works for everything and is the simplest option.
episodes.rule: Use preposition + que for relative clauses. Alternatives: cual, la cual, la que, quien. Que is sufficient for all cases.

examples.english: The girl I work with (with whom)

examples.spanish: La chica con la cual trabajo
examples.english: The girl I work with (with which)

examples.spanish: Es la chica con que trabajaba
examples.english: It's the girl I was working with

examples.spanish: La chica con que iba a la fiesta
examples.english: The girl I was going to the party with

episodes.notes: Just use que for everything at this stage. The other options (cual, quien, la que, la cual) will come naturally with exposure.

episodes.summary: Cuál means 'which' and is used in questions. You don't need the word 'one' - cuál alone means 'which one'.

examples.english: I don't know which one he wants
I don't know which one he wants to see

examples.spanish: No sé con cuál quiere hacerlo
examples.english: I don't know which one he wants to do it with

episodes.summary: Quién means who and pairs with he/she/it verb forms. Spanish has a plural form quiénes for when you know it's more than one person, which requires the they verb form.
episodes.rule: Quién + singular verb. Quiénes + plural verb. Who = he/she/it form.

examples.spanish: No sé con quién tengo que hacerlo
examples.english: I don't know who I have to do it with

examples.spanish: No sé para quién tengo que hacerlo
examples.english: I don't know who I have to do it for

examples.spanish: No sé por quién tengo que hacerlo
examples.english: I don't know who I have to do it because of

episodes.summary: The only difference in the entire Spanish language between -er and -ir verbs is the 'we' form in the present tense: -emos for -er verbs, -imos for -ir verbs. In all other forms and tenses, they behave identically.
episodes.rule: -er verbs: we = -emos (comemos). -ir verbs: we = -imos (vivimos). This is the ONLY difference between -er and -ir in all of Spanish.
episodes.notes: All other forms use the same endings: come/vive (he), comen/viven (they), etc.

episodes.summary: Lo + adjective means 'the [adjective] thing'. Lo interesante = the interesting thing. Lo fácil = the easy thing. Lo bueno = the good thing. Lo malo = the bad thing.
episodes.rule: Lo + adjective = the [adjective] thing. The adjective stays in masculine singular form.

examples.spanish: Lo interesante es que viven aquí
examples.english: The interesting thing is that they live here

examples.spanish: Lo interesante es que viven juntos
examples.english: The interesting thing is that they live together

examples.spanish: Lo fácil es que vienen juntos
examples.english: The easy thing is that they come together

examples.spanish: Lo complicado es que vienen juntos
examples.english: The complicated thing is that they come together

episodes.notes: Lo bueno = the good thing, lo malo = the bad thing. Very common and useful structure.

episodes.summary: Lo de means 'the thing about' or 'that business about'. Lo de la fiesta = that thing about the party. Lo de Juan = that business about Juan / Juan's business. Lo de mañana = that thing about tomorrow.
episodes.rule: Lo de + noun/name = the thing about [X], that matter of [X]

examples.spanish: No quiero hablar de lo de Juan así
examples.english: I don't want to talk about Juan's business like this
I want to organize that thing about tomorrow
But I don't want to organize it like this

episodes.notes: Lo de is used very often in Spanish. See it as a unit meaning 'that thing about'.

episodes.summary: Así means 'like this' or 'like that' and replaces the longer de esta forma / de esta manera. Very common in everyday Spanish.

examples.english: I don't want to organize it like this

episodes.notes: More common than de esta forma or de esta manera. You'll hear it all the time.

name: Preterite tense for -ar verbs (I and he/she)

episodes.summary: The preterite (dot/point in the past) for -ar verbs: remove -ar, add -é for I, -ó for he/she/it/usted. Both endings are accented. Note the I form uses -é (not -o), which is opposite to the present where I = -o.
episodes.rule: For -ar verbs: I = -é (hablé), he/she/it/usted = -ó (habló). Accented endings. Careful: I spoke = hablé (not habló). Present hablo (stress on HA-) vs preterite habló (stress on -BLÓ).

examples.english: I didn't send it to you yet

episodes.notes: The preterite is more messy/random than other tenses. The accent is critical: hablo (I speak, present) vs habló (he spoke, preterite).

episodes.summary: Recap of all three preterite forms for -ar verbs: hablé (I), habló (he/she), hablamos (we).
The reflexive se works in the preterite just like other tenses: se envió = it sent itself = it was sent. Don't add lo when using se - the se already completes the sentence.
episodes.rule: Se + preterite verb = passive meaning. Don't add lo/la with se.
episodes.notes: Resist the urge to say *se lo envió when you mean 'it was sent'. Se already completes the verb.

name: Preterite tense for -er/-ir verbs (I and he/she)

episodes.summary: For -er/-ir verbs in the preterite: remove ending, add -í for I, -ió for he/she/it/usted. Both endings are accented.
episodes.rule: For -er/-ir verbs: I = -í (comí), he/she/it/usted = -ió (comió). Ver is regular here: vi, vio.

examples.spanish: Lo vi, pero él no me vio
examples.english: I saw him, but he didn't see me

episodes.notes: Ver is regular in the preterite: vi, vio.

episodes.summary: Recap of all three preterite forms for -er/-ir verbs: comí (I), comió (he/she), comimos (we).
Que means what, that, AND than. For comparisons: más que = more than. After 'than', use subject pronouns (él, yo) not object pronouns, because the meaning is 'more than he ate', 'more than I ate'.
episodes.rule: Más + que = more than. Use subject pronouns after que (él, yo, not him, me).

examples.english: I ate more than him (more than he)
He ate more than me (more than I)

episodes.notes: English uses object pronouns after 'than' (more than him/me) which is actually peculiar. Spanish is more logical: más que él = more than he (ate).

episodes.summary: Nada means nothing. Spanish uses double negatives: no vi nada = I didn't see nothing = I didn't see anything.
episodes.rule: Double negative: no + verb + nada = didn't see anything

episodes.summary: Nadie means nobody. Can be combined with nada in double negative structures.
Durante means during and can also be used for 'for' with time periods, as an alternative to por.
episodes.rule: Durante = during, also works as 'for' with time. Alternative to por for time durations.

examples.english: He ate for half an hour
He spoke for half an hour

examples.spanish: Habló durante media hora sin parar
examples.english: He spoke for half an hour without stopping

episodes.notes: Durante comes from durar (to last) using the -ante adjective form. Can replace por for time periods.

episodes.summary: Sin means without. As a preposition, it takes the infinitive (sin parar = without stopping, not *sin parando).
episodes.rule: Sin + infinitive (not -ando/-iendo)

examples.spanish: Habló durante media hora sin parar
examples.english: He spoke for half an hour without stopping

episodes.summary: For -ar verbs, the preterite 'we' form is identical to the present (hablamos = we speak / we spoke). For -er verbs, present -emos becomes preterite -imos (comemos -> comimos). For -ir verbs, both present and preterite are -imos (vivimos stays vivimos). Context disambiguates.
episodes.rule: -ar: hablamos (present) = hablamos (preterite). -er: comemos (present) -> comimos (preterite). -ir: vivimos (present) = vivimos (preterite). The -er/-ir we-form difference in present (-emos vs -imos) means -er verbs change in preterite but -ir verbs don't.

examples.english: When we speak, I understand everything
When we spoke, I understood everything
We left / We leave (salir: same form)

episodes.notes: The ambiguity of hablamos (present or past) is resolved by context. Words like ayer, mañana, or the tense of surrounding verbs clarify the meaning.

episodes.summary: When describing past events, the imperfect (line) sets up an ongoing background action while the preterite (dot) marks a punctual event that interrupts or occurs within it. For example, 'I was cooking when the phone rang' uses imperfect for cooking and preterite for the phone ringing.
episodes.rule: Use imperfect for the ongoing/background action and preterite for the interrupting/punctual event in the same sentence, typically connected by 'cuando'.

examples.english: I was cooking when the phone rang.

examples.spanish: Hablaba por teléfono cuando María llegó.
examples.english: I was speaking on the phone when Maria arrived.

examples.spanish: Me parecía bien, pero cuando lo vi, no me gustó.
examples.english: It was seeming good to me, but when I saw it, I didn't like it.

episodes.summary: Practice of preterite forms for llegar and quedarse, reinforcing the -ar and reflexive verb patterns in the preterite.

Complete review of all preterite forms. For -ar verbs (hablar): hablé, habló, hablamos, hablaste, hablaron. For -er/-ir verbs (comer): comí, comió, comimos, comiste, comieron. The -er/-ir group has 'i' dominating (the inverse of the present tense where 'e' dominates).
episodes.rule: In the preterite, -er/-ir verbs have the /i/ sound everywhere except -ieron (where /e/ gets one appearance). This is the exact inverse of the present tense where /e/ dominates and /i/ appears only in -imos.

examples.english: I spoke, he spoke, we spoke, you spoke, they spoke.
I ate, he ate, we ate, you ate, they ate.

episodes.summary: Review of regular preterite endings. -ar verbs: -é, -ó, -amos, -aron, -aste. -er/-ir verbs: -í, -ió, -imos, -ieron, -iste.
episodes.notes: The informal 'you' preterite has both -s and -t- in the ending (-aste, -iste).

episodes.summary: Parecer works like gustar with indirect object pronouns. It can be used in both preterite (me pareció) and imperfect (me parecía) depending on framing.

examples.english: It was seeming good to me.

episodes.notes: Related to 'apparent' and 'aparecer' (to appear), and 'transparent'.

name: Se lo phonetic change (le lo -> se lo)

episodes.summary: When two object pronouns beginning with 'l' would appear together (le lo, les lo, le los, les los, le la, etc.), the first one changes to 'se'. This is purely a phonetic change -- Spanish does not like the sound of two l-words together.
episodes.rule: Any combination of le/les + lo/la/los/las becomes se + lo/la/los/las. This 'se' has no meaning change -- it is just a phonetic substitution.
episodes.notes: This 'se' is totally different from the reflexive 'se' (himself/herself). It is just a phonetic issue.

episodes.summary: When sentences with 'se lo' are ambiguous (could mean to him/her/them/you formal), you can clarify by adding 'a él', 'a ella', 'a ellos', 'a ellas', 'a usted', 'a ustedes' at the end. The structure is a repetition -- you keep the 'se' and add the clarification as an afterthought.
episodes.rule: With 'a' for clarification, always repeat the pronoun (le di algo a él -- keep the 'le' and add 'a él'). With other prepositions like 'para', do NOT repeat (lo compré para ti -- no 'me' needed).

examples.spanish: Él se lo envió a ella.

examples.english: He sent it to you guys.

examples.spanish: No sé si se lo envié.
examples.english: I don't know if I sent it to him.

episodes.notes: The 'a' used for clarification can represent other prepositions besides 'to' -- e.g., 'me lo compra a mí' could mean 'from me' or 'for me'. Because of this special flexible use, we always repeat with 'a'.

episodes.summary: Further practice of 'a + pronoun' clarification, now applied to the gustar construction. 'Le gusta' is ambiguous (he/she/you formal likes it), so 'a ella', 'a él', etc. are added to clarify.
episodes.notes: Be careful not to say 'ella le gusta' to mean 'she likes it' -- that would mean 'she is pleasing to [someone]', i.e., someone likes her. The 'a' is essential for correct meaning.

episodes.summary: Acordarse is a reflexive verb meaning 'to remember'. The o splits to ue when stressed (me acuerdo) but stays as o when unstressed (nos acordamos). The se becomes me/te/nos etc. depending on the subject.
episodes.rule: Acordarse: me acuerdo (I remember), te acuerdas, se acuerda, nos acordamos (no split -- unstressed), se acuerdan.

examples.spanish: No me acuerdo si se lo di a él o a ella.
examples.english: I don't remember if I gave it to him or to her.

examples.spanish: No nos acordamos si se lo dimos a él o a ellas.
examples.english: We don't remember if we gave it to him or to them (fem).

episodes.notes: The o does not split in 'nos acordamos' because the stress is not on the o.

episodes.summary: To express 'ago' in Spanish, use 'hace' (it makes) followed by a time expression. 'Two weeks ago' becomes 'hace dos semanas' (it makes two weeks).
episodes.rule: hace + [number] + [time unit] = [number] [time unit] ago

examples.english: I saw him two weeks ago.
I responded to her seven weeks ago.

examples.spanish: Se lo respondí hace un mes.
examples.english: I responded it to her one month ago.

episodes.summary: The preterite endings for 'you informal' (tú) are -aste for -ar verbs and -iste for -er/-ir verbs.
episodes.rule: -ar verbs: -aste (hablaste). -er/-ir verbs: -iste (comiste, saliste). Both contain 's' and 't' which relate to the informal you.

examples.english: Did you go out last night?
Why didn't you wait for me?

episodes.notes: Both 's' and 't' in the endings relate to informal 'you' and help you remember the form.

episodes.summary: The preterite endings for 'they' (ellos/ellas/ustedes) are -aron for -ar verbs and -ieron for -er/-ir verbs.
episodes.rule: -ar verbs: -aron (hablaron). -er/-ir verbs: -ieron (comieron).

episodes.summary: Practice deriving verbs from -ción nouns: discriminación -> discriminar.
Adding 'más' to negative words like nada or nadie gives the meaning of 'else': nada más (nothing else), nadie más (nobody else). 'Una vez más' means 'one more time'.
episodes.rule: nada más = nothing else / anything else; nadie más = nobody else / anybody else

examples.english: I want to see them one more time.

episodes.summary: Ver is irregular in the preterite: vi, vio, vimos, viste, vieron. The endings follow the -er/-ir pattern but the stem is just v-.
episodes.rule: vi, vio, vimos, viste, vieron

examples.english: Didn't you see me last night?

episodes.summary: Vez means 'time' in the sense of occasion (not clock time). It is feminine (una vez). 'Otra vez' means 'again' (another time). 'A la vez' means 'at the same time'.

examples.english: I want to see them again.
Did you go out to dance again?

examples.spanish: Todos llegaron a mi casa a la vez.
examples.english: Everybody arrived at my house at the same time.

episodes.notes: Vez is feminine (otra vez, not otro vez). V-e-z.

episodes.summary: Full review of possessive adjectives: mi/mis (my), tu/tus (your informal), nuestro/nuestra/nuestros/nuestras (our), su/sus (his/her/their/your formal). They agree with the thing possessed, not the possessor. 'Propio/a' means 'own'.
episodes.rule: Mi, tu, su change only for number (mi/mis, tu/tus, su/sus). Nuestro changes for both gender and number (nuestro/nuestra/nuestros/nuestras). Su/sus covers his, her, their, and your (formal) -- the plural 'sus' refers to plural things possessed, not plural possessors.

examples.english: I want to go to his/their houses.
We are with our friends. (all female)

examples.spanish: Lo vi con mis propios ojos.
examples.english: I saw it with my own eyes.

episodes.notes: Nuestro contains the o-split visible in nosotros. Avoid saying 'sus' just because you mean 'they' -- sus only for plural possessed items.

episodes.summary: In Spanish, you don't say 'my leg hurts' (mi pierna duele). Instead you say 'it hurts me the leg' (me duele la pierna). Spanish uses indirect object pronouns with body parts rather than possessive adjectives.
episodes.rule: For body part pain: [indirect object pronoun] + duele/duelen + la/el [body part]. NOT mi/tu/su + [body part] + duele.

examples.english: My leg hurts. (It hurts me the leg.)

examples.spanish: No me duele la pierna más.

episodes.notes: Doler has an o-split: duele (not 'dole'). This is part of a broader pattern where Spanish uses fewer possessive adjectives than English.

episodes.summary: Doler works like gustar -- it takes an indirect object pronoun. The o splits to ue when stressed (duele). The noun form is dolor (pain).

examples.english: I have a headache. (pain of head)

episodes.summary: Spanish expresses possession with 'de' (of) rather than the English 's. 'José's house' becomes 'la casa de José' (the house of José).
episodes.rule: [article] + [noun] + de + [owner] for possession. Like 'dolor de cabeza' (ache of the head).

examples.spanish: Vamos a la casa de José.
examples.english: We are going to José's house.

examples.spanish: Vamos a la fiesta de María.
examples.english: We are going to Maria's party.

episodes.summary: Solo means 'only' or 'just' in Spanish. It can be placed before or after the verb, with different emphasis.

examples.english: I spoke with you only. (more emphatic)

examples.spanish: No hablé con nadie más en la fiesta de María, solo hablé contigo.
examples.english: I didn't speak with anyone else at Maria's party, I only spoke with you.

name: Spanish stress and written accent rules

episodes.summary: Spanish has two natural stress rules: (1) words ending in a vowel, n, or s have stress on the penultimate (second-to-last) syllable; (2) words ending in any other consonant have stress on the final syllable. These endings (vowel, n, s) correspond to what verb forms end in. A written accent (tilde) appears only when a word breaks these natural rules.
episodes.rule: Ends in vowel/n/s -> stress penultimate syllable (no accent needed). Ends in other consonant -> stress final syllable (no accent needed). Written accent = the word breaks the rule above.

examples.english: End in vowel/n/s, stress on penultimate -- no written accent
End in -r (not vowel/n/s), stress on final syllable -- no written accent
End in -n but stress is on final syllable -- breaks rule, so written accent on ó
End in vowel but stress on final syllable -- preterite 3rd person always has accent
End in vowel, stress on end -- written accent
Lemon -- ends in n but stress on final syllable, so accent on ó
Health -- ends in d, stress on end, follows rule, no accent

episodes.notes: The rule is easy to remember by looking at present tense verbs: they end in vowels, n's, or s's, and always stress the penultimate syllable (with the one exception of estar: estás, están, está which have written accents).

episodes.summary: Some one-syllable words that are spelled the same are differentiated by a written accent. The accent does not change pronunciation -- it only distinguishes meaning in writing.
episodes.rule: Monosyllable pairs: sí (yes) vs si (if); mí (me, after prepositions) vs mi (my); tú (you) vs tu (your); sé (I know) vs se (reflexive/phonetic pronoun).

examples.english: Yes I want it. (sí with accent = yes)
If I want it. (si without accent = if)
I give it to him. (se without accent)

episodes.summary: Question words take a written accent when used as questions, but not when used as non-question conjunctions/relative words.
episodes.rule: dónde (where?) vs donde (where, relative); cómo (how?) vs como (like/as)

examples.spanish: Voy a verlo donde vimos el fútbol.
examples.english: I'm going to see him where we saw the football. (not a question -- no accent)

episodes.notes: Many native Spanish speakers make mistakes with these accents in writing.

episodes.summary: Full paradigm of this/that/these/those: este/esta (this), ese/esa (that), estos/estas (these), esos/esas (those). Derived from 'esta noche' as a hook. The neuter forms esto/eso are used for general/non-specific references (not tied to a specific noun).
episodes.rule: Masculine: este/ese (singular), estos/esos (plural). Feminine: esta/esa (singular), estas/esas (plural). Neuter (general, no specific noun): esto/eso. To get 'that' from 'this', remove the t: esta -> esa, este -> ese.

examples.spanish: Nada me gustó más que eso.
examples.english: Nothing pleased me more than that.

episodes.notes: Spanish also allows post-positioned demonstratives: 'el hombre este', 'el hombre ese' -- possibly a structure influenced by Arabic during 800 years of contact.

episodes.summary: Cuánto means 'how much'. It agrees in gender and number with what it modifies: cuántas veces (how many times, feminine because vez is feminine).
episodes.rule: Cuánto/cuánta/cuántos/cuántas -- agrees with the noun it modifies.

examples.spanish: No sé cuántas veces lo visité.
examples.english: I don't know how many times I visited him.

examples.spanish: No sé cuántas veces lo visitaron.
examples.english: I don't know how many times they visited him.

examples.spanish: No sé cuántas veces la visitaron.
examples.english: I don't know how many times they visited her.

episodes.notes: To determine gender of 'vez', look back to where it was used: 'otra vez' (not otro vez) confirms it is feminine.

episodes.summary: The subjunctive ('mood tense') expresses mood/subjectivity. It exists in English too (e.g., 'it is important that you be here', 'had I gone') but is used much more extensively in Spanish. Formation is simple: switch verb groups. -ar verbs use -er/-ir endings; -er/-ir verbs use -ar endings.
episodes.rule: Formation: -ar verbs switch to -e endings (habla -> hable, hablan -> hablen, hablamos -> hablemos). -er/-ir verbs switch to -a endings (come -> coma, comen -> coman). The I form and he/she/it/you(formal) form are the same in the subjunctive (e.g., hable = I or he/she/it/you formal).
episodes.notes: Uses are divided into obligatory (must use, considered an error otherwise) and optional (gives different flavor/meaning). The I form becomes the same as he/she/it/you(formal) because switching o for o gives no change.

episodes.summary: Recap of subjunctive structures using dejar: after quiero que (desire), after cuando (future reference), after es esencial que (judgment), and after ojalá (hope). The subjunctive conveys desire/order vs the indicative which states fact.
episodes.rule: Quiero que + subjunctive (desire/order). Quiero decirle que + subjunctive = I want to tell him to... vs que + indicative = I want to tell him that he is...

examples.spanish: Quiero que me dejes un mensaje
examples.english: I want you to leave me a message

examples.spanish: Quiero decirle que me deje un mensaje
examples.english: I want to tell him to leave me a message

examples.spanish: Cuando te deje el mensaje, llámame
examples.english: When he leaves you the message, call me

examples.spanish: Es esencial que vea mi mensaje
examples.english: It's essential that he sees my message

episodes.notes: Using indicative after decir que states a fact (he IS leaving a message). Using subjunctive conveys an order/desire (to leave a message).

episodes.summary: When expressing 'I want you to...' in Spanish, the structure is 'quiero que + subjunctive'. This is an obligatory use of the subjunctive -- using the indicative here is considered an error. English says 'I want you to wait'; Spanish says 'I want that you wait (subjunctive)'.
episodes.rule: quiero que + [subjunctive verb]. The subjunctive is obligatory after quiero que, quieres que, etc.

examples.english: I want you to wait for him/it.
Do you want me to wait for you?
Do you want me to wait for you? (clarified with yo)

episodes.notes: The subjunctive form after que is ambiguous for I/he/she/you(formal) -- clarify with yo, él, ella, usted if needed.

episodes.summary: When 'what' is not a question (i.e., used as a relative pronoun meaning 'that which'), Spanish uses 'lo que' instead of 'qué'.
episodes.rule: qué = what (question); lo que = what (not a question / relative pronoun)

examples.spanish: No quiero que hables sobre lo que hablamos.
examples.english: I don't want you to speak about what we spoke about.

examples.spanish: No quiero que hables sobre lo que hablábamos.
examples.english: I don't want you to speak about what we were speaking about.

examples.spanish: Necesito que prepare lo que falta.
examples.english: I need you to prepare what's missing.

episodes.summary: Like 'quiero que', 'necesito que' (I need that...) is followed by the subjunctive. The pattern is: a verb expressing subjectivity/desire + que + subjunctive.
episodes.rule: necesito que + [subjunctive verb]

examples.english: I need you (formal) to prepare it.
I need you (formal) to prepare it. (clarified)

examples.spanish: Necesito que prepare lo que falta.
examples.english: I need you to prepare what's missing.

episodes.summary: Faltar means to be short of, to be missing, to lack. The noun form is 'falta' (lack). The expression 'hace falta' means 'it is necessary' and is followed by the subjunctive when used with 'que'.
episodes.rule: hace falta que + [subjunctive] = it is necessary that...

examples.english: It is necessary that we eat soon.

episodes.notes: 'Hace falta' literally means 'it makes lack' -- an extremely common expression, another way of saying 'it's necessary'.

episodes.summary: Preferir que (I prefer that...) also triggers the obligatory subjunctive. The key is the feeling: you are casting your subjectivity onto the world.
episodes.rule: prefiero que + [subjunctive verb]

examples.english: I prefer that he stays. / I prefer him to stay.

examples.spanish: Prefiero que no se queden aquí.
examples.english: I prefer that they don't stay here.

examples.spanish: Prefiero que se lo des a él.
examples.english: I prefer you to give it to him.

examples.spanish: Prefiero que se lo des a ella.
examples.english: I prefer you to give it to her.

episodes.summary: English words ending in -ity become -idad in Spanish.

name: Contar (to count / to tell / to recount)

episodes.summary: Contar means both 'to count' and 'to recount/tell' (especially stories). It has an o -> ue stem change: cuento (I tell/count). The noun 'cuento' means 'story'. 'Contar con' means 'to count on' (to count with).

examples.english: I want you to tell me. (informal)
I want you (plural) to tell me something.
I want you to count on me.

episodes.notes: Contar con = to count on (literally 'to count with'). English says 'count on', Spanish says 'count with'. Spanish speakers speaking English may say 'you can count with me' -- negative language transfer that reveals the Spanish structure.

name: Subjunctive after esperar que (I hope that)

episodes.summary: When 'esperar' is used to mean 'to hope', 'espero que' triggers the subjunctive, just like 'quiero que' and 'necesito que'.
episodes.rule: espero que + [subjunctive verb]

examples.english: I hope that you count on me.
We hope that they count on Maria.

examples.spanish: Esperamos que lo cuenten a María.
examples.english: We hope that they tell it to Maria.
I hope that they see Maria.

episodes.summary: The subjunctive of ver is built on the I-form 'veo' (not 'ven'), giving vea/vean/veamos. This avoids ambiguity with 'van' (they go) from ir.
episodes.rule: Subjunctive of ver: vea (I/he/she/you formal), veas (you informal), veamos (we), vean (they/you plural).

examples.english: I hope that you understand David.
I hope that you (formal) understand David.

episodes.notes: If the subjunctive were formed regularly from 'ven', it would give 'van' which would cause confusion with ir (to go). So the subjunctive is based on veo -> vea.

episodes.summary: When a specific human is the direct object of a verb (having the action done to them) and no other preposition is present, Spanish uses 'a' before that person. This exists because Spanish word order is flexible, so without the personal 'a', it would be unclear whether the person is the subject or object. 'A + el' contracts to 'al'.
episodes.rule: Use personal 'a' when: (1) a specific human is the direct object, (2) no other preposition is already present. Not used with indefinite/non-specific humans (e.g., 'I need to find a plumber' -- no personal a). 'A' + 'el' = 'al'.

examples.english: I hope that they see Maria.
I hope that you understand David.
I want him to see the man.
I want the man to see.

examples.spanish: Me gusta que visite a la profesora.
examples.english: I like that he visits the teacher.

examples.spanish: Me gusta que la profesora visite.
examples.english: I like that the teacher visits.

episodes.notes: Can be used to personify non-humans (e.g., 'escapar a la muerte' personifies death). Generally used with specific humans, not non-specific ones.

episodes.summary: Commands in Spanish use the subjunctive mood in most cases: all negative commands, all formal commands, and all plural commands. The subjunctive gives the command form. For positive informal (tú) commands, a shortcut is used instead: drop the -s from the present tense tú form.
episodes.rule: Negative commands (all persons): use subjunctive. Formal commands: use subjunctive. Plural commands: use subjunctive. Positive informal commands: drop -s from present tú form (compras -> compra, comes -> come).

examples.spanish: No se lo des a él.

episodes.summary: With positive commands, object pronouns (me, lo, se, etc.) attach to the end of the verb as one word. With negative commands, pronouns go before the verb in their normal position.
episodes.rule: Positive command: verb + pronoun(s) as one word (e.g., espérenme, cómalo, cómpramelo). Negative command: no + pronoun(s) + verb (e.g., no lo coma, no los esperen).
episodes.notes: The pronoun placement with positive commands helps the ear recognize it as a command, since the vowel switch alone is a subtle change.

episodes.summary: For positive informal (tú) commands only, instead of using the subjunctive, Spanish drops the -s from the tú present tense form. This is a shortcut because telling people informally what to do is extremely common.
episodes.rule: compras -> compra (Buy!), comes -> come (Eat!), olvidas -> olvida (Forget!). The result looks identical to the he/she/it form but is derived by dropping -s from tú.

examples.english: Don't sell it. (informal -- back to subjunctive)

episodes.notes: Only applies to POSITIVE INFORMAL commands. Negative informal commands still use the subjunctive (no lo comas, not *no lo come).

episodes.summary: Brief review: for positive informal commands, drop the -s from the tú form (esperas -> espera). Contrasted with 'esperemos' (let's wait) and 'no esperemos' (let's not wait).
episodes.notes: '¿Esperamos?' with rising intonation sounds like 'shall we wait?' rather than a command.

episodes.summary: Olvidar means to forget. It needs an object (what is forgotten). If no object is stated, use the reflexive olvidarse (me olvido). Related to English 'oblivion'.
episodes.notes: Related to English 'oblivion'. The verb doesn't like to be alone -- needs an object or reflexive.

name: Callar / callarse (to quiet / to shut up)

episodes.summary: Callar means to quiet; callarse means to shut up (reflexive). Not polite.
episodes.notes: Cállate uses the informal positive shortcut. Cállense uses the subjunctive because it's plural.

episodes.summary: To express 'let him/her/it/them do something' without using quiero que or prefiero que, you can simply say 'que' followed by the subjunctive. This conveys wishes or commands directed at third parties.
episodes.rule: que + [subjunctive] = let him/her/it/them [verb]

name: Subjunctive after cuando (when referring to future)

episodes.summary: After 'cuando', if you refer to a future event (something that hasn't happened yet), you must use the subjunctive. If 'cuando' refers to a general/habitual situation, use the indicative. This reflects an unwillingness to state a future event as fact.
episodes.rule: cuando + [indicative] = general/habitual. cuando + [subjunctive] = future event. The main clause after cuando can use normal present tense since the future context is already established.

examples.english: When we eat together, we speak. (general)
When we eat together (future), we will speak.
When we speak. (future -- whenever we do speak)
We see her when we go. (general)
We will see her when we go. (future)

episodes.notes: The language seems to reflect a kind of superstition -- uncomfortable referring to something in the future as a fact, so the subjunctive marks it as not-yet-realized.

episodes.summary: When cuando refers to a future event that hasn't happened yet, the subjunctive is used. When cuando refers to something general/habitual, the present indicative is used.
episodes.rule: Cuando + subjunctive for future events; cuando + indicative for general/habitual.

episodes.summary: The subjunctive of ir is irregular. The base is 'vaya' (v-a-y-a). Forms: vaya, vayas, vayamos, vayan.
episodes.rule: Subjunctive of ir: vaya (I/he/she/you formal), vayas (you informal), vayamos (we), vayan (they/you plural).

examples.english: We will see her when we go.

name: E to I stem change (elegir type)

episodes.summary: Some verbs like elegir have an e that turns into i (rather than splitting to ie) when stressed. Elijo (I choose), but elegimos (we choose -- unstressed, no change).
episodes.rule: When the stem e is stressed, it becomes i (not ie). When unstressed, it stays as e. Example: elijo, eliges, elige, elegimos, eligen.

examples.spanish: No quiero que elijas por mí.
examples.english: I don't want you to choose for me (on my behalf).

episodes.notes: Related to 'elect'. Election = elección.

episodes.summary: After 'para que' (so that / in order that), the subjunctive is obligatory. Note: 'para + infinitive' (in order to) does NOT use the subjunctive. The subjunctive is needed only when there is a 'que' and a different subject.
episodes.rule: para que + [subjunctive]. But para + [infinitive] = in order to (no subjunctive).

examples.english: We have cooked so that they eat.
We have met up in order to speak. (no que, no subjunctive)

examples.spanish: Lo he hecho para que vuelvas.
examples.english: I have done it so that you come back. (informal)

examples.spanish: Lo he hecho para que vuelvan.
examples.english: I have done it so that they come back.

examples.spanish: Lo han hecho para que volvamos.
examples.english: They have done it so that we come back.

examples.spanish: Hemos hecho todo para que tú también vuelvas.
examples.english: We have done everything so that you also come back.

episodes.notes: Para can be shortened to 'pa' in some dialects: 'pa que volvamos'.

name: Volver (to return / to go back / to come back)

episodes.summary: Volver means to return/go back/come back. The o splits to ue when stressed (vuelvo, vuelves). Related to 'revolve'; 'revolver' in Spanish means to stir.
episodes.notes: Related to 'revolve' in English. In Spanish, 'revolver' means to stir.

episodes.summary: Romper is to break (transitive); romperse is to break (intransitive -- something breaks by itself). Practiced with the subjunctive: 'no quiero que se rompa' (I don't want it to break).

examples.english: I don't want it to break.
I don't want him/her to break it.

episodes.notes: Related to rapture, corrupt, disrupt, erupt -- the -rupt root.

episodes.summary: Hecho is the irregular past participle of hacer. Also means 'fact' as a noun.

examples.spanish: Lo he hecho para que vuelvas.
examples.english: I have done it so that you come back.

episodes.summary: También means 'also' or 'as well'. It contains 'tan' (so/as) + 'bien' (well), making it a near-direct translation of the English 'as well'. The n of tan becomes m before the b.

examples.english: So that you too come back.

examples.spanish: Hemos hecho todo para que tú también vuelvas.
examples.english: We have done everything so that you also come back.

episodes.notes: también = tan (as/so) + bien (well). The n softened to m before the b. A near-direct translation of English 'as well'.

episodes.summary: Encontrarse (reflexive of encontrar) means 'to meet up' -- literally 'to find oneself/each other'.

examples.english: We have met up to speak.

episodes.summary: The informal positive command of hacer is irregular: 'haz' (not 'hace'). H-a-z.
episodes.rule: Informal positive command of hacer = haz (irregular).
episodes.notes: In most of Spain, pronounced /hath/.

episodes.summary: An optional use of the subjunctive: putting the verb after donde/como/lo que into the subjunctive changes the meaning from 'where/how/what' to 'wherever/however/whatever'. This is not obligatory -- the indicative version is correct too, just with a different meaning.
episodes.rule: donde + indicative = where; donde + subjunctive = wherever. como + indicative = how; como + subjunctive = however. lo que + indicative = what; lo que + subjunctive = whatever. cuando + indicative (general) vs cuando + subjunctive = whenever.
episodes.notes: This is optional -- 'haz lo que prefieres' is perfectly correct Spanish, meaning 'do what you prefer'. The subjunctive version adds the 'ever' nuance.

episodes.summary: Seguir means to follow and also to continue/carry on. It has the same e->i stem change as elegir: sigo (I follow), sigue (he follows), but seguimos (we follow -- unstressed, no change). Related verbs: conseguir (to get/manage to), perseguir (to chase).
episodes.rule: Stem change e->i when stressed: sigo, sigues, sigue, seguimos, siguen. Same pattern in related verbs: consigo, persigo.

examples.english: I'll follow you wherever you go.

episodes.notes: Related verbs share the irregularity: conseguir (to get/manage to) -> consigo; perseguir (to chase) -> persigo.

episodes.summary: The subjunctive of ser is irregular with the base 'sea'. Forms: sea, seas, seamos, sean. The expression 'o sea' (literally 'or it might be') is a very common filler word, like 'uhh' or 'I mean'.
episodes.rule: Subjunctive of ser: sea (I/he/she/you formal), seas (you informal), seamos (we), sean (they/you plural).

examples.english: I prefer it to be like this.
Whatever will be, will be. / Whatever it is.
I mean... / In other words... (filler expression)

episodes.notes: 'O sea' is extremely common in spoken Spanish as a discourse filler.

episodes.summary: Hay means both 'there is' and 'there are' — it does not change. It comes from haber. Había is the past form and also does not change for singular or plural.
episodes.rule: Hay = there is / there are (invariable). Había = there was / there were (invariable). When using 'there has to be' (exist), use haber, not ser or estar.

examples.spanish: Hay dos personas aquí que hablan español
examples.english: There are two people here that speak Spanish

examples.spanish: Había dos personas aquí que hablaban español
examples.english: There were two people here that spoke Spanish

examples.spanish: Tiene que haber algo que puedas hacer
examples.english: There has to be something that you can do

episodes.notes: When 'be' means 'exist' (there has to be something), use haber — not ser or estar.

episodes.summary: When 'be' means 'exist', use haber not ser/estar. 'There should be something' = debería haber algo.
episodes.rule: Check if 'be' means 'exist' — if so, use haber.

examples.english: There should be something to eat

episodes.notes: Your first instinct will be to choose ser or estar. If neither fits, check if 'be' means 'exist' — then it's haber.

episodes.summary: When questioning the existence of something unknown using hay + que, the subjunctive is used. When stating what exists as fact, the indicative is used.
episodes.rule: ¿Hay alguien que + subjunctive? (existence uncertain) vs. Hay alguien que + indicative (existence stated as fact)

examples.english: Is there somebody here that speaks Spanish?

examples.spanish: Hay dos personas aquí que hablan español
examples.english: There are two people here that speak Spanish

examples.spanish: Tiene que haber algo que puedas hacer
examples.english: There has to be something that you can do

episodes.notes: The subjunctive has structural limitations — it occurs in certain places structurally and only to give certain meanings. Its use can vary by dialect and idiolect.

episodes.summary: When using the structure 'es/está + adjective + que + verb', the verb goes into the subjunctive. However, using 'si' (if) instead of 'que' does not trigger the subjunctive.
episodes.rule: Es/está + adjective + que + subjunctive. But: es/está + adjective + si + indicative.

examples.english: It's terrible that you eat this
It's terrible if you eat this

examples.spanish: Es posible que vayamos más tarde
examples.english: It's possible that we go later
It's not necessary that I respond

examples.spanish: Es importante que lo encontremos pronto
examples.english: It's important that we find it soon

episodes.notes: The 'que' is what introduces the mood tense in this structure. With 'si' instead of 'que', no subjunctive is needed.

episodes.summary: Qué can be used like 'how' in exclamations: qué fuerte (how heavy/hardcore), qué bueno (how good), qué increíble (how incredible). When followed by que, the subjunctive continues.
episodes.rule: Qué + adjective = How + adjective! Qué + adjective + que + subjunctive.

examples.english: How hardcore that he is here

episodes.summary: No hace falta que + subjunctive is an alternative way to say 'it's not necessary that'. Hacer falta literally means 'to make lack'.
episodes.rule: No hace falta que + subjunctive = it's not necessary that

examples.english: It's not necessary that I respond

episodes.notes: Common expression; alternative to 'no es necesario que'.

episodes.summary: After seguir (to continue/follow), use the gerund (-ando/-iendo) to express 'continue doing something'.
episodes.rule: Seguir + gerund = to continue doing. Sigo hablando = I continue speaking.

examples.english: It's probable that he continues speaking

examples.spanish: Es imperativo que siga siendo gratis
examples.english: It's imperative that it continues being free

episodes.summary: Empezar and comenzar need 'a' to connect to an infinitive (empezar a hablar = to start to speak). Using the gerund instead changes meaning to 'start out doing' (empezar hablando = to start out speaking, right from the beginning).
episodes.rule: Empezar/comenzar + a + infinitive = to start to do. Empezar/comenzar + gerund (no a) = to start out doing.

examples.spanish: Es espectacular que comiencen a hablar español
examples.english: It's spectacular that they start to speak Spanish

examples.spanish: Es espectacular que comiencen hablando español
examples.english: It's spectacular that they start out speaking Spanish

episodes.notes: Empezar and comenzar are among a handful of non-movement verbs that need a preposition (a) to connect to an infinitive.

episodes.summary: Terminar uses 'de' to connect to an infinitive (terminar de vender = to finish selling). Using the gerund instead gives the meaning 'end up doing' (terminar vendiendo = to end up selling).
episodes.rule: Terminar + de + infinitive = to finish doing. Terminar + gerund = to end up doing.

examples.spanish: Quiero que hablen conmigo cuando terminen de venderlos
examples.english: I want you guys to speak with me when you finish selling them

examples.spanish: Quiero que hablen conmigo cuando terminen vendiéndolos
examples.english: I want you guys to speak with me when you end up selling them

episodes.notes: If a verb needs a preposition to join the infinitive, that's a clue it may also take the gerund with a different meaning.

episodes.summary: Ojalá means 'hopefully' and is followed by the subjunctive. It has Arabic origins related to 'Allah', reflecting 800 years of Muslim influence on Spanish.
episodes.rule: Ojalá + subjunctive = hopefully (something happens)
episodes.notes: Possibly from 'ojo-Allah' (eye of God). Very common expression. After ojalá, use subjunctive.

episodes.summary: English words ending in -tial become -cial in Spanish, similar to the -tion to -ción pattern.
episodes.rule: -tial in English → -cial in Spanish

episodes.summary: Review of three uses of se: (1) reflexive se meaning 'self' (se queda = he stays/remains), (2) phonetic se replacing les before lo/la/los/las (se lo preparo = I prepare it for them), and (3) NEW: se + indirect object pronoun to express 'on me/you/him' — something happening to someone.
episodes.rule: Se + me/te/le/nos/les + verb = something happens 'on' someone (unintentionally/affecting them). Order: se + indirect object + verb.

examples.spanish: La batería siempre se me acaba
examples.english: The battery always runs out on me
Does the battery run out on you? (informal)
Does the battery run out on you? (formal)
The battery runs out on you guys/them

episodes.notes: Uses le (not lo) because it's 'on you' / 'to you'. This 'on me' structure is much more common in Spanish than English and not restricted to a few verbs.

episodes.summary: The past subjunctive is formed from the 'they' form of the preterite (point in the past). Change the final -o of -aron/-ieron to -a to get -aran/-ieran (they). Remove -n for I/he/she. Add -s for informal you. Add -mos (with accent) for we. One form serves both preterite and imperfect contexts.
episodes.rule: They preterite (e.g. hablaron) → change -on to -an = hablaran (they, past subjunctive). Drop -n = hablara (I/he/she). Add -s = hablaras (you informal). Add -mos = habláramos (we).

examples.english: I wanted them to eat with us
I was hoping that he would call me
We needed him to speak with us

examples.spanish: Volví a llamarte para que organizáramos
examples.english: I called you again so that we organized

episodes.notes: The past subjunctive is the same for both line and point contexts. In English you'd say 'would call' but in Spanish you use the past subjunctive instead.

episodes.summary: A present-tense trigger (es relevante que) can be followed by either present or past subjunctive depending on the time being referred to. Past subjunctive is used when referring back to something in the past.
episodes.rule: Present trigger + past subjunctive is valid when referring to a past event: No es muy relevante que lo encontraran.

examples.spanish: No es muy relevante que lo encontraran
examples.english: It's not very relevant that they found it

examples.spanish: No es muy relevante que lo encuentren
examples.english: It's not very relevant that they find it

examples.spanish: Era muy relevante que lo encontraran
examples.english: It was very relevant that they found it

examples.spanish: Es increíble que volvieran a hacerlo
examples.english: It's incredible that they did it again

episodes.summary: Volver a + infinitive means 'to do something again'. It uses 'a' because volver is a verb of movement. It's very common in Spanish.
episodes.rule: Volver a + infinitive = to do again. No quiero que vuelva a pasar = I don't want it to happen again.

examples.spanish: No quiero que la puerta vuelva a cerrarse
examples.english: I don't want the door to close again

examples.spanish: No quiero que vuelva a pasar
examples.english: I don't want it to happen again

episodes.notes: An alternative to 'otra vez' for saying 'again'.

name: Para que + subjunctive vs para + infinitive

episodes.summary: Para + infinitive (in order to do) does not require subjunctive. Para que + conjugated verb requires subjunctive. You can restructure sentences to use para + infinitive to avoid the subjunctive.
episodes.rule: Para + infinitive = no subjunctive needed. Para que + verb = subjunctive required.

examples.english: I called you again in order to organize

examples.spanish: Volví a llamarte para que organizáramos
examples.english: I called you again so that we organized

episodes.notes: You can sometimes restructure a sentence with para + infinitive to avoid the subjunctive.

episodes.summary: Puerto (port) and puerta (door) are related. Stress the English 'port', change vowel to get puerto; change -o to -a to get puerta.
episodes.notes: Port → puerto (stress the o) → puerta (change o to a).

episodes.summary: The imperfect (line in the past) of ser is irregular: era. The mnemonic is the English word 'era' — a line in the past is an era.
episodes.rule: Ser imperfect: era (I/he/she/you formal), eras (you informal), éramos (we), eran (they)
episodes.notes: Remember: an 'era' is a line in the past. Pronounce the English word 'era' as Spanish and you get the form.

episodes.summary: Lo + adjective means 'the [adjective] thing': lo importante = the important thing, lo increíble = the incredible thing. Using 'lo + adjective + es que' avoids the subjunctive that 'es + adjective + que' would trigger.
episodes.rule: Lo + adjective + es que + indicative (no subjunctive). Contrast: Es + adjective + que + subjunctive.

examples.english: The incredible thing is that they return (no subjunctive)

examples.spanish: Lo increíble es que vuelven a hacerlo
examples.english: The incredible thing is that they do it again

examples.spanish: Es increíble que vuelvan a hacerlo
examples.english: It's incredible that they do it again (subjunctive)

episodes.notes: A useful strategy to avoid the subjunctive: restructure 'es + adj + que' into 'lo + adj + es que'.

name: Que + past subjunctive at start of sentence

episodes.summary: Starting a sentence with 'que + past subjunctive' gives the meaning 'the fact that you may have...' or 'that you might have...'. This expresses a mood/attitude about a past event.
episodes.rule: Que + past subjunctive + rest of sentence = the fact that (someone) may have (done something)...

examples.spanish: Que lo compraras no es mi problema
examples.english: That you bought it / the fact that you may have bought it is not my problem

examples.spanish: Que le contaras no es mi problema
examples.english: That you told him is not my problem

episodes.summary: After 'si' (if) referring to hypothetical past situations, Spanish uses the past subjunctive. This mirrors the English 'if I were you'. The result clause uses the conditional (would).
episodes.rule: Si + past subjunctive, + conditional. Si pudiera cocinar, cocinaría = If I could cook, I would cook.

examples.english: If I could cook, I would cook
If I could cancel it, I would cancel it

episodes.notes: English has a remnant of this: 'if I were you' instead of 'if I was you'.

episodes.summary: For counterfactual past conditions ('if you had done X'), use si + past subjunctive of haber + past participle. The result uses the conditional perfect (habría + past participle). Haber in the preterite uses the root hub-.
episodes.rule: Si + hubiera/hubieras/hubiéramos + past participle, + habría + past participle. Hub- is the preterite root of haber.

examples.spanish: Si me lo hubieras contado, habría ido
examples.english: If you had told me, I would have gone
If we had known, we would have gone

episodes.summary: Poder becomes pud- in the preterite (pudieron). Haber becomes hub- (hubieron). These irregular stems carry over into the past subjunctive.
episodes.rule: Poder → pud- (pudieron, pudiera). Haber → hub- (hubieron, hubiera).

episodes.summary: Si vieras su casa = if you saw their house. A common expression using past subjunctive of ver after si for hypothetical situations.

examples.english: If you saw it, you would understand it

episodes.summary: An alternative form of the past subjunctive exists using -ase/-iese instead of -ara/-iera: hablase instead of hablara, comiese instead of comiera. Usage depends on dialect. No difference in meaning.
episodes.rule: -ara/-iera can be replaced with -ase/-iese. Hablara = hablase. Comiera = comiese. Comiéramos = comiésemos.
episodes.notes: You don't need to use this form, but you should recognize it. The -ara/-iera form is more common.

episodes.summary: A group of irregular verbs use -go for the I form in the present tense, but only for I — all other persons are normal (or have their own stem changes). The verbs include: tener (tengo), venir (vengo), salir (salgo), poner (pongo), decir (digo), hacer (hago), oír (oigo), traer (traigo).
episodes.rule: Go verbs: -go only appears in the I form. He/she/they etc. conjugate normally (tiene, viene, sale, etc.).

name: Go verbs in the subjunctive (-go to -ga)

episodes.summary: In the present subjunctive, go verbs change -go to -ga as the base for ALL persons (unlike the present where -go is only for I). So tengo → tenga, vengo → venga, traigo → traiga, etc.
episodes.rule: -go → -ga for subjunctive base. Tenga (I/he/she), tengas (you informal), tengamos (we), tengan (they).

examples.english: I want you to bring it
I don't want you to distract me
Tell me (formal) — used when answering the phone in Spain

episodes.notes: Venga is used colloquially in Spain as 'come on'. Dígame is used to answer the phone in Spain.
