episodes.summary: Go verbs use -ga as the subjunctive base. Practiced with commands (formal uses subjunctive), negative commands, and after cuando referring to the future.

examples.english: Tell me when you come (future reference)
Tell me when you do it (formal, future reference)

episodes.summary: Traer means 'to bring', but in compound verbs it translates as -tract: extraer (extract), atraer (attract), contraer (contract), distraer (distract). All follow the same -go pattern (distraigo, etc.).
episodes.rule: Traer in compounds = -tract in English. All compounds follow the traigo/-traiga pattern.

episodes.summary: For informal positive commands, go verbs simply drop the -go ending: tengo → ten, vengo → ven, salgo → sal, digo → di, pongo → pon. This is different from regular verbs which drop the -s from the tú form.
episodes.rule: Informal positive command of go verbs: drop -go. Tengo → ten. Vengo → ven. Salgo → sal. Digo → di. Formal commands still use -ga form.
episodes.notes: Dime and dígame are both heard when answering the phone in Spain. Venga has lost its formal feeling in Spain — it's used colloquially as 'come on' by everyone.

episodes.summary: With positive commands, object pronouns attach to the end of the verb. With negative commands, pronouns go before the verb.
episodes.rule: Positive command: verb + pronoun (póngalo). Negative command: no + pronoun + verb (no lo ponga).

name: Go verbs: irregular future and conditional

episodes.summary: Go verbs contract in the future and conditional tenses. Most replace the final vowel of the infinitive with -d-: tener → tendr-, venir → vendr-, poner → pondr-, salir → saldr-. Then add the normal future (-é, -ás, -á, -emos, -án) or conditional (-ía, -ías, -ía, -íamos, -ían) endings.
episodes.rule: Most go verbs: infinitive vowel → d. Tener → tendr-. Venir → vendr-. Poner → pondr-. Salir → saldr-. Exception: traer is regular (traeré). Hacer → har-. Decir → dir-.

examples.spanish: Vendríamos más tarde, pero no tenemos tiempo
examples.english: We would come later, but we don't have time
Are you going out tonight? (present with future context)

episodes.notes: The -go in the present tense is an alarm bell that the verb will be irregular in the futures too. Traer is an exception — it stays regular.

episodes.summary: Hacer and decir are extra irregular in the future/conditional. Hacer → har- (haré, haría). Decir → dir- (diré, diría). Once you know one form, you can derive all the others.
episodes.rule: Hacer → har- (haré, harás, hará, haremos, harán / haría, harías, etc.). Decir → dir- (diré, dirás, etc. / diría, dirías, etc.).

examples.spanish: Lo haríamos, pero estamos ocupados ahora
examples.english: We would do it, but we're busy now
I tell you later (present with future context)

episodes.summary: Poder is not a go verb but also contracts in the future/conditional: the -e- falls out giving podr- (podré, podría). Similar contraction to haber → habr-.
episodes.rule: Poder → podr- (podré, podría). The /e/ falls out.

examples.english: I could / I would be able to
We could / we would be able to

episodes.notes: Subtle difference in sound: podría (I could) vs pondría (I would put).

episodes.summary: Review of future endings added to the infinitive: -é (I), -ás (you informal), -á (he/she/you formal), -emos (we), -án (they). These match the irregular present forms of haber.
Review of conditional endings added to the infinitive: -ía as the key sound, plus -mos, -n, -s for other persons.
episodes.notes: Remember 'me gustaría' (I would like) as the hook for the -ía sound.

episodes.summary: The imperfect is very regular with only three irregular verbs: ser (era), ir (iba), and ver (veía). Ser uses 'era' (remember: an era is a line in the past). Ir uses iba (a mix of -aba and -ía). Ver keeps the extra -e from veo: veía.
episodes.rule: Ser: era, eras, éramos, eran. Ir: iba, ibas, íbamos, iban. Ver: veía, veías, veíamos, veían.
episodes.notes: The imperfect is the safest tense — almost entirely regular. You can fall back to it to avoid irregular preterite forms.

episodes.summary: Irregular preterite verbs share three traits: (1) the stem changes, (2) they use -e and -o endings (from -ar) for I and he/she, but -imos/-iste/-ieron (from -er/-ir) for the rest, and (3) the endings are NOT accented. Once you know the irregular stem, all endings follow the same pattern.
episodes.rule: Irregular preterite: changed stem + -e (I), -o (he/she), -imos (we), -iste (you informal), -ieron (they). No accent on endings.
episodes.notes: You can use the imperfect (tenía, estaba) to avoid irregular preterite forms when you need to get meaning across quickly.

episodes.summary: More irregular preterite stems covered: poner → pus- (puse, puso), hacer → hic-/hiz- (hice, hizo), venir → vin- (vine, vino), traer → traj- (traje, trajo). All follow the same ending pattern: -e (I), -o (he/she), -imos (we), -iste (you), -ieron (they), unaccented.
episodes.rule: Poner → pus-. Hacer → hic- (hizo for he/she, z replaces c before o). Venir → vin-. Traer → traj-.
episodes.notes: Poner → pus- reveals why compound forms use -pose in English (componer/compose, exponer/expose). Hizo uses z instead of c to preserve the /s/ sound before o.

name: Ser and ir share the same preterite

episodes.summary: Ser (to be) and ir (to go) share the same preterite forms: fui (I was/went), fue (he was/went), fuimos (we), fuiste (you informal), fueron (they). Context determines meaning. Extremely irregular — doesn't follow the standard pattern.
episodes.rule: Fui = I was / I went. Fue = he/she was / went. Fuimos, fuiste, fueron.
episodes.notes: The -i- drops from -ieron in fueron (because 'fuieron' is hard to say). Same happens with trajeron and dijeron.

name: Irregular preterite stems carry into past subjunctive

episodes.summary: The past subjunctive is built from the 'they' preterite form, so all irregular stems carry over: fueron → fuera, tuvieron → tuviera, hicieron → hiciera, dijeron → dijera, trajeron → trajera.
episodes.rule: They preterite → past subjunctive. Fueron → fuera. Tuvieron → tuviera. Hicieron → hiciera.

examples.spanish: Si lo tuviera, te lo contaría
examples.english: If I had it, I would tell you
I wanted them to do it
I wanted us to do it

episodes.notes: This is why the past subjunctive is built from the 'they' preterite: to carry over irregularities.

episodes.summary: When the irregular preterite stem ends in j or the diphthong would be hard to pronounce, the -i- drops from -ieron: trajieron → trajeron, dijieron → dijeron, fuieron → fueron.
episodes.rule: After j or difficult diphthongs, -ieron loses the i: trajeron, dijeron, fueron.
episodes.notes: The -i- falls away for efficiency of pronunciation.

episodes.summary: Demasiado (too/too many) agrees in gender and number when modifying a noun (demasiadas casas, demasiados coches), but stays invariable as demasiado when modifying an adjective (demasiado agresivo, demasiado agresiva, demasiado agresivos).
episodes.rule: Demasiado + noun = agrees (demasiadas casas, demasiados coches). Demasiado + adjective = invariable (demasiado agresivo/a/os/as).
episodes.notes: The key is identifying word type: noun → agreement, adjective → no agreement.

name: El with feminine words starting/ending in a

episodes.summary: Feminine two-syllable words that begin and end with 'a' take 'el' instead of 'la' to avoid the awkward 'la a-a' sound: el ala (the wing), el agua (the water), el aula (the classroom). They remain grammatically feminine (el agua fría, not frío).
episodes.rule: Two-syllable feminine words beginning and ending with 'a' take 'el' (not 'la'). They remain feminine for adjective agreement.

examples.english: The carpet (not affected — more than two syllables)

episodes.notes: This doesn't apply to longer words like la alfombra or la almohada — only two-syllable words beginning and ending in a.

episodes.summary: Uno (one), alguno (some), and ninguno (none) lose the final -o when placed before a masculine noun: un hombre, algún coche, ningún coche. The feminine forms (una, alguna, ninguna) do not change.
episodes.rule: Before a masculine noun: uno → un, alguno → algún, ninguno → ningún. Feminine unchanged: una, alguna, ninguna.
episodes.notes: The -o drops only before masculine nouns. When standalone (no noun following), the full form is used.

episodes.summary: The lesson emphasizes analyzing patterns in Spanish you encounter, identifying what type of word is involved (noun vs adjective), and making your own rules. Understanding why something is correct — not just memorizing — is the path to controlling the language.
episodes.notes: Identify word types (noun, adjective, verb) to figure out most grammatical behaviors. Analyze, don't memorize.

name: Leísmo (le/les for direct objects in Spain)

episodes.summary: In most of Spain, le and les are used when referring to people as direct objects (not just indirect objects). So 'le veo' means 'I see him/her' in Spain, while 'lo veo' means 'I see it'. In most of Latin America, lo/la/los/las are used for direct objects regardless.
episodes.rule: In most of Spain: le/les for people (direct object), lo/la for things. In Latin America: lo/la/los/las for all direct objects.
episodes.notes: A dialectal feature — not a rule to follow, but to recognize.

episodes.summary: Vosotros is the informal plural 'you' used in most of Spain. It is not used in Latin America, where ustedes serves for both formal and informal plural.
episodes.notes: Only used in Spain. Something to listen for if practicing with Spanish dialect speakers.

episodes.summary: Vos is an alternative to tú used in the Río de la Plata region (Buenos Aires, Montevideo) and around 30% of the Spanish-speaking world. Verb conjugations change slightly — the accent shifts to the final syllable, which prevents stem vowel splits: puedes → podés, quieres → querés.
episodes.rule: Vos conjugations: accent on final syllable. Puedes → podés. Quieres → querés. The stressed final vowel prevents the stem from splitting.

examples.english: You can (vos form, instead of puedes)
You want (vos form, instead of quieres)

episodes.notes: Used exclusively or mixed with tú depending on region. The accent on the end prevents stem changes (o doesn't split to ue, e doesn't split to ie).

episodes.summary: Many Spanish accents aspirate the s sound, producing /h/ instead of /s/: está → /ehtá/, espero → /ehpero/, cómo está → /cómo ehtá/.
episodes.notes: Common in many dialects. Something to recognize, not necessarily adopt.

name: Listening comprehension as a separate skill

episodes.summary: Listening is a separate skill from speaking. At first, you will understand less than you can produce. The key is to deconstruct what you hear (the reverse of building sentences), analyze it, and insist — the language will appear to slow down over time.
episodes.notes: Watch movies with subtitles, match English subtitles to Spanish audio. Music is good practice. Don't be overwhelmed — insist and analyze.
